EpiCLim_Differential_expression_analysis_Transcriptomic_F0_Liver

Manon

2026-03-03

Setup

Load count matrix

The raw count matrix was produced by the nf-core/rnaseq pipeline using STAR alignment and Salmon quantification, then summarized to gene-level counts using tximeta. Salmon produces non-integer counts, these were rounded to integers as required by DESeq2. Gene names and Ensembl IDs are stored separately at this stage: gene IDs are used as row names throughout the analysis (unique identifiers), while gene names are retained for annotation of results later.

counts <- openxlsx::read.xlsx("Raw_counts_transcripto_liver.xlsx")

# Save gene names separately for annotation later
genes_names<-counts$gene_name
counts$gene_name<-NULL

# Set gene_id as rownames
rownames(counts)<-counts$gene_id
counts$gene_id<-NULL

# Round counts to integers (remove decimals)
counts<-round(counts)

# Confirm the dimensions of the final matrix with rows = genes and columns = samples. 
dim(counts)
## [1] 32520    39

Load metadata

The metadata file contains one row per sample with all relevant biological and technical variables knowns: condition, sex, exposure batch, RIN score, duplication rate, 5’-3’ bias, and proper pairs percentage. Setting sample names as row names is important as it allows direct indexing between the count matrix and metadata throughout the analysis. The final check confirms that every sample in the count matrix has a corresponding entry in the metadata –> must return TRUE before proceeding further.

metadata <- openxlsx::read.xlsx("metadata.xlsx")

# Set sample names as row names for direct indexing with count matrix
rownames(metadata) <- metadata$sample

# Check that all sample names match with the count matrix
all(colnames(counts) %in% metadata$sample)
## [1] TRUE

Data exploration

Before running the differential expression analysis, we assessed the technical quality of each sample to identify potential outliers and understand the sources of variation in the dataset. An initial inspection of the MultiQC report from the nf-core/rnaseq pipeline flagged three samples with elevated technical metrics: * Ctrl8F3: duplication rate 70.5%, 5’-3’ bias 1.74, proper pairs 21.9%, mean insert size 950bp * Acid18F2: duplication rate 67.5%, 5’-3’ bias 1.90 (the highest in the dataset), proper pairs 26.2%, mean insert size 701bp * AcidPFOS67F2: duplication rate 64.8%, 5’-3’ bias 1.80, proper pairs 26.4%

These flags are noted here but exclusion decisions will be made later based on a consistent set of criteria applied across all samples.

As a first step, we created a minimal DESeq2 object with an intercept-only design (~ 1) purely for exploratory purposes. No differential expression was computed at this stage. This allows us to estimate size factors, which reflect each sample’s effective library size after accounting for composition biases. Size factors are then added to the metadata so they can be used as a variable in downstream PCA plots.

To understand technical variability across samples, we tested whether size factor, duplication rate, 5’-3’ bias, and proper pairs were correlated with each other. If they correlate, it suggests one underlying library quality issue affecting all four metrics simultaneously rather than four independent problems, simplifying how we interpret and account for technical variation in the dataset.

#Create a simple DESeq2 object for exploration
dds_explore<-DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(countData = counts,
                                    colData = metadata,
                                    design= ~ 1) # ~1 means no biological model -> QC check only
## converting counts to integer mode
# Estimate size factors which reflect the library size per sample
dds_explore <- estimateSizeFactors(dds_explore)

# Add the size factors to the metadata
metadata$size_factor <- sizeFactors(dds_explore)[rownames(metadata)]

# Identify samples with extreme size factors -> extreme values need investigation
metadata[order(metadata$size_factor, decreasing = TRUE), 
         c("condition", "sex", "size_factor", "duplication_rate")]
##              condition    sex size_factor duplication_rate
## PFOSTemp71M2  PFOSTemp   male   4.3405265             18.1
## Ctrl8F2           Ctrl female   4.1787768             17.2
## APT85M3            APT   male   2.8831553             33.8
## Temp35F2          Temp female   2.4361764             41.1
## APT92M2            APT   male   2.1389609             37.9
## Acid21F3          Acid female   2.0238848             32.3
## PFOSTemp74M3  PFOSTemp   male   1.6557491             39.7
## Ctrl7F3           Ctrl female   1.4418010             45.8
## PFOSTemp80F3  PFOSTemp female   1.3245612             38.3
## AcidTemp56F2  AcidTemp female   1.2861756             47.6
## APT88M3            APT   male   1.2731618             42.2
## AcidPFOS61M3  AcidPFOS   male   1.2604184             54.7
## Temp33F2          Temp female   1.2099646             49.4
## APT101F2           APT female   1.1973540             52.2
## AcidPFOS62M3  AcidPFOS   male   1.1133723             48.0
## Acid15M3          Acid   male   1.0602767             50.3
## Temp27M3          Temp   male   1.0529824             45.5
## PFOS43F2          PFOS female   1.0384709             57.9
## PFOSTemp81F3  PFOSTemp female   0.9948715             46.0
## PFOSTemp79F3  PFOSTemp female   0.9534384             55.7
## Temp31F3          Temp female   0.9411495             54.9
## Ctrl6F2           Ctrl female   0.9213929             49.1
## Temp26M3          Temp   male   0.9076428             53.7
## AcidPFOS65F2  AcidPFOS female   0.8811573             54.6
## AcidPFOS67F2  AcidPFOS female   0.7389415             64.8
## AcidPFOS69F3  AcidPFOS female   0.7343492             48.2
## PFOS43F3          PFOS female   0.7128537             64.1
## APT92F3            APT female   0.6821463             55.1
## AcidTemp56F3  AcidTemp female   0.6794231             52.8
## PFOS42F3          PFOS female   0.6705504             57.2
## Ctrl3M3           Ctrl   male   0.6545095             68.4
## AcidTemp55F2  AcidTemp female   0.6545091             55.0
## APT93F3            APT female   0.6324522             45.8
## Ctrl9F3           Ctrl female   0.6137274             58.3
## Ctrl7F2           Ctrl female   0.5710378             64.8
## Ctrl8F3           Ctrl female   0.5457508             70.5
## Acid18F2          Acid female   0.5249951             67.5
## Temp32F3          Temp female   0.4470945             62.7
## Acid20F3          Acid female   0.4175537             62.3
# Check correlation between size factor and sequencing quality metrics -> strong correlation indicate 
cor.test(metadata$size_factor, metadata$duplication_rate)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  metadata$size_factor and metadata$duplication_rate
## t = -11.025, df = 37, p-value = 3.004e-13
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.9332712 -0.7738460
## sample estimates:
##      cor 
## -0.87558
cor.test(metadata$size_factor, metadata$five_three_prim_bias)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  metadata$size_factor and metadata$five_three_prim_bias
## t = -3.7422, df = 37, p-value = 0.0006179
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.7204038 -0.2497655
## sample estimates:
##        cor 
## -0.5239894
cor.test(metadata$size_factor, metadata$proper_pairs)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  metadata$size_factor and metadata$proper_pairs
## t = 7.0115, df = 37, p-value = 2.736e-08
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  0.5774750 0.8647774
## sample estimates:
##       cor 
## 0.7553626
cor.test(metadata$duplication_rate, metadata$five_three_prim_bias)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  metadata$duplication_rate and metadata$five_three_prim_bias
## t = 6.2221, df = 37, p-value = 3.144e-07
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  0.5159567 0.8408697
## sample estimates:
##       cor 
## 0.7150659
cor.test(metadata$duplication_rate, metadata$proper_pairs)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  metadata$duplication_rate and metadata$proper_pairs
## t = -15.299, df = 37, p-value < 2.2e-16
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.9625509 -0.8683143
## sample estimates:
##        cor 
## -0.9292474

All four metrics: duplication rate, proper pairs percentage, 5’-3’ bias, and size factor significantly correlate with each other, confirming that a single technical axis drives variation across multiple quality measurements in this dataset. Samples with high duplication rates tend to have low size factors, higher 5’-3’ bias, and low proper pairs. DESeq2 accounts for this through size factor normalisation where each sample’s counts are divided by its size factor before statistical testing, correcting for differences in effective library size between samples.

Principal component analysis

PCA was performed on rlog-transformed counts to visualise the main sources of variation across all 39 samples. We used blind = TRUE for the rlog transformation at this exploration stage, meaning the transformation is calculated without knowledge of the experimental design. This is appropriate for QC and outlier detection where we want an unbiased view of sample relationships.

Each principal component was then correlated with both biological variables (condition, sex, exposure batch) and technical variables (RIN, duplication rate, size factor, 5’-3’ bias, proper pairs) to identify what drives sample separation. For continuous variables Pearson correlation was used, while for condition ANOVA was used. This approach ensured that decisions about sample exclusion and model design were based on evidence rather than visual inspection alone.

# rlog transformation (prefer to vst here due to low sample size and high variability) 
rld <- rlog(dds_explore, blind = TRUE) 
## rlog() may take a few minutes with 30 or more samples,
## vst() is a much faster transformation
# Extract PCA coordinates for the top 500 most variable genes
pca_data <- plotPCA(rld, intgroup = c("condition"), returnData = TRUE)
## using ntop=500 top features by variance
# Add all biological and technical variables for correlation testing
pca_data$exposure       <- metadata[pca_data$name, "exposure"]
pca_data$RIN            <- metadata[pca_data$name, "RIN"]
pca_data$size_factor    <- metadata[pca_data$name, "size_factor"]
pca_data$dup_rate       <- metadata[pca_data$name, "duplication_rate"]
pca_data$bias_5_3       <- metadata[pca_data$name, "five_three_prim_bias"]
pca_data$proper_pairs   <- metadata[pca_data$name, "proper_pairs"]

# Get the variance explained by each PC
percentVar <- round(100 * attr(pca_data, "percentVar"))
cat("PC1 explains:", percentVar[1], "%\n")
## PC1 explains: 76 %
cat("PC2 explains:", percentVar[2], "%\n")
## PC2 explains: 6 %
# Create a shared theme for all PCA plots
theme_pca <- theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"))

# Axis labels for all plots
x_lab <- paste0("PC1 (", percentVar[1], "%)")
y_lab <- paste0("PC2 (", percentVar[2], "%)")

# --- PCA according to biological variables ---

# COnditions
p_condition <- ggplot(pca_data, 
                      aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, 
                          color = condition, 
                          shape = sex,
                          label = name)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(x = x_lab, y = y_lab, title = "A — Condition") +
  theme_pca

# ANOVA tests to see if the conditions separate on each PC
summary(aov(PC1 ~ condition, data = pca_data))
##             Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)
## condition    7   7200    1029   0.316  0.941
## Residuals   31 100845    3253
summary(aov(PC2 ~ condition, data = pca_data))
##             Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)
## condition    7   1409   201.3   0.947  0.485
## Residuals   31   6587   212.5
# Sex
p_sex <- ggplot(pca_data, 
                aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, 
                    color = sex,
                    label = name)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(x = x_lab, y = y_lab, title = "B — Sex") +
  theme_pca

# Correlation
cor.test(pca_data$PC1, as.numeric(as.factor(pca_data$sex)))
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_data$PC1 and as.numeric(as.factor(pca_data$sex))
## t = -2.1286, df = 37, p-value = 0.04001
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.58485992 -0.01649483
## sample estimates:
##        cor 
## -0.3302929
cor.test(pca_data$PC2, as.numeric(as.factor(pca_data$sex)))
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_data$PC2 and as.numeric(as.factor(pca_data$sex))
## t = 7.587, df = 37, p-value = 4.751e-09
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  0.6164381 0.8792745
## sample estimates:
##       cor 
## 0.7802074
# Exposure batch
pca_data$exposure <- as.factor(metadata[pca_data$name, "exposure"])
p_exposure <- ggplot(pca_data, 
                     aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, 
                         color = exposure,
                         label = name)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(x = x_lab, y = y_lab, title = "C — Exposure batch") +
  theme_pca

# Correlation
cor.test(pca_data$PC1, as.numeric(as.factor(pca_data$exposure)))
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_data$PC1 and as.numeric(as.factor(pca_data$exposure))
## t = -2.9384, df = 37, p-value = 0.005652
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.6599267 -0.1384671
## sample estimates:
##        cor 
## -0.4349804
cor.test(pca_data$PC2, as.numeric(as.factor(pca_data$exposure)))
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_data$PC2 and as.numeric(as.factor(pca_data$exposure))
## t = 0.26097, df = 37, p-value = 0.7956
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.2763908  0.3535991
## sample estimates:
##       cor 
## 0.0428643
# Figure 1 — One plot for all biological variables
ggarrange(p_condition, p_sex, p_exposure,
          ncol = 2, nrow = 2)

# --- PCA according to technical variables ---

# RIN
p_RIN <- ggplot(pca_data, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = RIN)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "red", high = "blue") +
  theme_pca +
  ggtitle("PCA colored by RIN")

# Correlation
cor.test(pca_data$PC1, pca_data$RIN)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_data$PC1 and pca_data$RIN
## t = -0.37774, df = 37, p-value = 0.7078
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.3702570  0.2585934
## sample estimates:
##         cor 
## -0.06198069
cor.test(pca_data$PC2, pca_data$RIN)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_data$PC2 and pca_data$RIN
## t = -4.0601, df = 37, p-value = 0.0002442
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.7409039 -0.2905428
## sample estimates:
##       cor 
## -0.555167
# Duplication 
p_duplication <- ggplot(pca_data, 
                aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, 
                    color = dup_rate,
                    shape = sex)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "blue", high = "red") +
  labs(x = x_lab, y = y_lab, title = "E — Duplication rate") +
  theme_pca

# Correlation
cor.test(pca_data$PC1, pca_data$dup_rate)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_data$PC1 and pca_data$dup_rate
## t = -2.6055, df = 37, p-value = 0.01314
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.63088033 -0.08931718
## sample estimates:
##        cor 
## -0.3937383
cor.test(pca_data$PC2, pca_data$dup_rate)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_data$PC2 and pca_data$dup_rate
## t = -3.1276, df = 37, p-value = 0.003427
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.6753493 -0.1656517
## sample estimates:
##        cor 
## -0.4572691
# Size factors
p_sf <- ggplot(pca_data, 
               aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, 
                   color = size_factor,
                   shape = sex)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "purple", high = "yellow") +
  labs(x = x_lab, y = y_lab, title = "F — Size factor") +
  theme_pca

# Correlation
cor.test(pca_data$PC1, pca_data$size_factor)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_data$PC1 and pca_data$size_factor
## t = 3.5804, df = 37, p-value = 0.0009811
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  0.2282834 0.7092629
## sample estimates:
##       cor 
## 0.5072636
cor.test(pca_data$PC2, pca_data$size_factor)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_data$PC2 and pca_data$size_factor
## t = 3.5306, df = 37, p-value = 0.001129
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  0.2215747 0.7057337
## sample estimates:
##       cor 
## 0.5019968
# 5'-3' bias
p_bias <- ggplot(pca_data, 
                 aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, 
                     color = bias_5_3,
                     shape = sex)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "blue", high = "red") +
  labs(x = x_lab, y = y_lab, title = "G — 5'-3' bias") +
  theme_pca

# Correlation
cor.test(pca_data$PC1, pca_data$bias_5_3)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_data$PC1 and pca_data$bias_5_3
## t = -1.2166, df = 37, p-value = 0.2315
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.4818285  0.1272646
## sample estimates:
##        cor 
## -0.1961278
cor.test(pca_data$PC2, pca_data$bias_5_3)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_data$PC2 and pca_data$bias_5_3
## t = -1.67, df = 37, p-value = 0.1034
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.53553654  0.05538846
## sample estimates:
##        cor 
## -0.2647555
# Proper pairing
p_ppairs <- ggplot(pca_data, 
               aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, 
                   color = proper_pairs,
                   shape = sex)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "red", high = "blue") +
  labs(x = x_lab, y = y_lab, title = "H — Proper pairs") +
  theme_pca

# Correlation
cor.test(pca_data$PC1, pca_data$proper_pairs)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_data$PC1 and pca_data$proper_pairs
## t = 1.4899, df = 37, p-value = 0.1447
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.08391596  0.51477707
## sample estimates:
##       cor 
## 0.2378999
cor.test(pca_data$PC2, pca_data$proper_pairs)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_data$PC2 and pca_data$proper_pairs
## t = 3.9896, df = 37, p-value = 0.0003007
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  0.2816639 0.7365105
## sample estimates:
##       cor 
## 0.5484411
# Figure 2 — all technical variables in one plot
ggarrange(p_RIN, p_duplication, p_sf, p_bias, p_ppairs,
          ncol = 2, nrow = 3)

# Build a correlation summary table across all variables and both PCs
pca_cor_table <- data.frame(
  Variable = c("Condition","Sex", "Exposure batch", "RIN", "Duplication rate", "Size factor", "5'-3' bias",
               "Proper pairs"),
  PC1_cor_score = c(0.316,-0.330, -0.435,-0.062, -0.394, 0.507, -0.196, 0.238),
  PC1_p_value = c(0.941, 0.040, 0.006,0.708, 0.013, 0.001, 0.232, 0.145),
  PC1_sig = c("ns", "*", "**", "ns", "*", "**", "ns", "ns"),
  PC2_r_cor_score = c(0.947,0.780, 0.043, -0.555,-0.457, 0.502, -0.265, 0.548),
  PC2_p_value = c(0.485, "4.751e-09", 0.796, 0.0002, 0.003, 0.001, 0.103, 0.0003),
  PC2_sig = c("ns", "***", "ns", "***", "**", "**", "ns", "***"),
  Overall_significance = c("none","strong","moderate","strong","strong","strong","none","strong"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

kable(pca_cor_table,
      col.names = c("Variable", "r/F", "p-value", "Sig.",
                    "r/F", "p-value", "Sig.", "Overall significance"),
      caption = "Correlation of technical and biological variables 
      with principal components (rlog transformation, n=39), Strong = significant in both PCs or |r| > 0.5 in one PC, Moderate = significant in one PC with |r| 0.3–0.5, None = not significant in either PC.",
      align = c("l","r","r","c","r","r","c","l")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE) %>%
  add_header_above(c(" " = 1,
                     "PC1 (76%)" = 3,
                     "PC2 (6%)" = 3,
                     " " = 1))
Correlation of technical and biological variables with principal components (rlog transformation, n=39), Strong = significant in both PCs or |r| > 0.5 in one PC, Moderate = significant in one PC with |r| 0.3–0.5, None = not significant in either PC.
PC1 (76%)
PC2 (6%)
Variable r/F p-value Sig. r/F p-value Sig. Overall significance
Condition 0.316 0.941 ns 0.947 0.485 ns none
Sex -0.330 0.040
0.780 4.751e-09 *** strong
Exposure batch -0.435 0.006 ** 0.043 0.796 ns moderate
RIN -0.062 0.708 ns -0.555 2e-04 *** strong
Duplication rate -0.394 0.013
-0.457 0.003 ** strong
Size factor 0.507 0.001 ** 0.502 0.001 ** strong
5’-3’ bias -0.196 0.232 ns -0.265 0.103 ns none
Proper pairs 0.238 0.145 ns 0.548 3e-04 *** strong

PC1 explained 76% of total variance and was significantly associated with both biological and technical variables: sex, exposure batch, and size factors. PC2 explained 6% of variance and was strongly driven by sex, RIN, duplication rate, size factor, and proper pairs. The absence of condition separation on either PC is expected at this stage since the dataset includes both sexes, and sex is known to be a major driver of hepatic gene expression in zebrafish. The strong sex effect on PC2 justified splitting the analysis by sex for all downstream differential expression analyses.

Females analysis

The analysis was restricted to female samples for all downstream differential expression analyses. Males were excluded at this stage due to insufficient sample size per condition for reliable statistical modelling (n = 11 males). The female subset contains 28 samples across the 8 conditions.

meta_females <- metadata[metadata$sex == "female", ]
counts_females <- counts[, rownames(meta_females)]

# Quick check to confirm sample numbers and condition distribution across the exposure replicates
cat("Number of female samples:", nrow(meta_females), "\n")
## Number of female samples: 28
cat("\nCondition distribution:\n")
## 
## Condition distribution:
print(table(meta_females$condition, meta_females$exposure))
##           
##            2 3
##   Acid     1 2
##   AcidPFOS 2 1
##   AcidTemp 2 1
##   APT      1 2
##   Ctrl     3 3
##   PFOS     1 2
##   PFOSTemp 0 3
##   Temp     2 2

We have an unbalanced distribution across exposure replicates with PFOSTemp that has all three samples from exposure 3, while several conditions have only one sample in one of the two replicates.

PCA females

A new rlog transformation and set of PCA were performed on the female subset only to get a cleaner view of variation within females without the sex effect seen in the full dataset. The same systematic approach was used: correlating each PC with biological and technical variables to understand what drives sample separation.

# New DESEQ2 object for females only
dds_f <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(
  countData = counts_females,
  colData   = meta_females,
  design    = ~ 1
)
## converting counts to integer mode
# rlog trasnformation
rld_f <- rlog(dds_f, blind = TRUE)

# Extraction of PCA coordinates
pca_f <- plotPCA(rld_f,
                 intgroup = c("condition", "sex"),
                 returnData = TRUE)
## using ntop=500 top features by variance
# Add the metadata variables
pca_f$RIN          <- as.numeric(meta_females[pca_f$name, "RIN"])
pca_f$exposure     <- as.factor(meta_females[pca_f$name, "exposure"])
pca_f$size_factor  <- as.numeric(meta_females[pca_f$name, "size_factor"])
pca_f$dup_rate     <- as.numeric(meta_females[pca_f$name, "duplication_rate"])
pca_f$bias_5_3     <- as.numeric(meta_females[pca_f$name, "five_three_prim_bias"])
pca_f$proper_pairs <- as.numeric(meta_females[pca_f$name, "proper_pairs"])

# Get the variance explained by each PC
percentVar_f <- round(100 * attr(pca_f, "percentVar"))
cat("PC1 explains:", percentVar[1], "%\n")
## PC1 explains: 76 %
cat("PC2 explains:", percentVar[2], "%\n")
## PC2 explains: 6 %
# Update the labels for plots axis
x_lab_f <- paste0("PC1 (", percentVar_f[1], "%)")
y_lab_f <- paste0("PC2 (", percentVar_f[2], "%)")

# --- PCA according to biological variables ---

# Conditions in females
p_condition_f <- ggplot(pca_f, 
                      aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, 
                          color = condition, 
                          shape = sex,
                          label = name)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(x = x_lab, y = y_lab, title = "A — Condition") +
  theme_pca

# ANOVA
summary(aov(PC1 ~ condition, data = pca_f))
##             Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)
## condition    7  12723    1818   0.684  0.684
## Residuals   20  53158    2658
summary(aov(PC2 ~ condition, data = pca_f))
##             Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)
## condition    7  855.9   122.3   0.957  0.488
## Residuals   20 2556.1   127.8
# Exposure batch in females
pca_f$exposure <- as.factor(metadata[pca_f$name, "exposure"])
p_exposure_f <- ggplot(pca_f, 
                     aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, 
                         color = exposure,
                         label = name)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(x = x_lab, y = y_lab, title = "C — Exposure batch") +
  theme_pca

# Correlation
cor.test(pca_f$PC1, as.numeric(as.factor(pca_f$exposure)))
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_f$PC1 and as.numeric(as.factor(pca_f$exposure))
## t = -1.5253, df = 26, p-value = 0.1393
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.59594783  0.09684159
## sample estimates:
##        cor 
## -0.2865896
cor.test(pca_f$PC2, as.numeric(as.factor(pca_f$exposure)))
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_f$PC2 and as.numeric(as.factor(pca_f$exposure))
## t = -2.0415, df = 26, p-value = 0.05147
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.654067952  0.001610294
## sample estimates:
##        cor 
## -0.3716899
# Figure 3 - plots PCA biological variables females
ggarrange(p_condition_f, p_exposure_f, ncol = 2)

# --- PCA according to technical variables ---

#RIN
p_RIN_f <- ggplot(pca_f, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = RIN)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "red", high = "blue") +
  theme_pca +
  ggtitle("PCA colored by RIN")

# Correlation
cor.test(pca_f$PC1, pca_f$RIN)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_f$PC1 and pca_f$RIN
## t = -1.4607, df = 26, p-value = 0.1561
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.5880465  0.1088775
## sample estimates:
##        cor 
## -0.2753855
cor.test(pca_f$PC2, pca_f$RIN)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_f$PC2 and pca_f$RIN
## t = 0.22094, df = 26, p-value = 0.8269
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.3352012  0.4097487
## sample estimates:
##        cor 
## 0.04328933
# Duplication rate in females
p_duplication_f <- ggplot(pca_f, 
                aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, 
                    color = dup_rate,
                    shape = sex)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "blue", high = "red") +
  labs(x = x_lab, y = y_lab, title = "E — Duplication rate") +
  theme_pca

# Correlation
cor.test(pca_f$PC1, pca_f$dup_rate)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_f$PC1 and pca_f$dup_rate
## t = -2.9546, df = 26, p-value = 0.006569
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.7366508 -0.1578011
## sample estimates:
##        cor 
## -0.5013618
cor.test(pca_f$PC2, pca_f$dup_rate)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_f$PC2 and pca_f$dup_rate
## t = 0.57729, df = 26, p-value = 0.5687
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.2719961  0.4660148
## sample estimates:
##       cor 
## 0.1124964
# Size factors in females
p_sf_f <- ggplot(pca_f, 
               aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, 
                   color = size_factor,
                   shape = sex)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "purple", high = "yellow") +
  labs(x = x_lab, y = y_lab, title = "F — Size factor") +
  theme_pca

# Correlation
cor.test(pca_f$PC1, pca_f$size_factor)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_f$PC1 and pca_f$size_factor
## t = 4.2904, df = 26, p-value = 0.0002185
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  0.3563376 0.8199517
## sample estimates:
##       cor 
## 0.6438237
cor.test(pca_f$PC2, pca_f$size_factor)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_f$PC2 and pca_f$size_factor
## t = 0.10629, df = 26, p-value = 0.9162
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.3549967  0.3908781
## sample estimates:
##        cor 
## 0.02084031
# 5'-3' bias females
p_bias_f <- ggplot(pca_f, 
                 aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, 
                     color = bias_5_3,
                     shape = sex)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "blue", high = "red") +
  labs(x = x_lab, y = y_lab, title = "G — 5'-3' bias") +
  theme_pca

# Correlation
cor.test(pca_f$PC1, pca_f$bias_5_3)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_f$PC1 and pca_f$bias_5_3
## t = -1.3545, df = 26, p-value = 0.1872
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.5747632  0.1286616
## sample estimates:
##       cor 
## -0.256739
cor.test(pca_f$PC2, pca_f$bias_5_3)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_f$PC2 and pca_f$bias_5_3
## t = 1.3508, df = 26, p-value = 0.1884
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.1293571  0.5742893
## sample estimates:
##       cor 
## 0.2560782
# Proper pairs females
p_ppairs_f <- ggplot(pca_f, 
               aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, 
                   color = proper_pairs,
                   shape = sex)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "red", high = "blue") +
  labs(x = x_lab, y = y_lab, title = "H — Proper pairs") +
  theme_pca

# Correlation
cor.test(pca_f$PC1, pca_f$proper_pairs)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_f$PC1 and pca_f$proper_pairs
## t = 2.5528, df = 26, p-value = 0.0169
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  0.08956259 0.70329402
## sample estimates:
##       cor 
## 0.4476808
cor.test(pca_f$PC2, pca_f$proper_pairs)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_f$PC2 and pca_f$proper_pairs
## t = -0.76912, df = 26, p-value = 0.4488
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.4946988  0.2371225
## sample estimates:
##        cor 
## -0.1491488
# Arrange all technical plots together
ggarrange(p_RIN_f,p_duplication_f, p_sf_f, p_bias_f, p_ppairs_f, ncol = 2, nrow = 4)

# Build the correlation summary table
pca_cor_table_f <- data.frame(
  Variable = c("Condition","Exposure batch", "RIN", "Duplication rate", "Size factor", "5'-3' bias", 
               "Proper pairs"),
  PC1_cor_score = c(0.684,-0.287, -0.275,-0.501, 0.644, -0.257, 0.448),
  PC1_p_value = c(0.684, 0.139, 0.156, 0.007, 0.0002, 0.187, 0.017),
  PC1_sig = c("ns", "ns", "ns", "*", "**", "ns", "*"),
  PC2_r_cor_score = c(0.957,-0.372, 0.043, 0.112, 0.021, 0.256, -0.149),
  PC2_p_value = c(0.488, 0.051, 0.827, 0.569, 0.916, 0.188, 0.449),
  PC2_sig = c("ns", "borderline", "ns", "ns", "ns", "ns", "ns"),
  Overall_significance = c("none","bordeline","none","strong","strong","none","moderate"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

kable(pca_cor_table_f,
      col.names = c("Variable", "r/F", "p-value", "Sig.",
                    "r/F", "p-value", "Sig.", "Overall significance"),
      caption = "Correlation of technical and biological variables 
      with principal components (rlog transformation, n=28), Strong = significant in both PCs or |r| > 0.5 in one PC, Moderate = significant in one PC with |r| 0.3–0.5, None = not significant in either PC.",
      align = c("l","r","r","c","r","r","c","l")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE) %>%
  add_header_above(c(" " = 1,
                     "PC1 (76%)" = 3,
                     "PC2 (6%)" = 3,
                     " " = 1))
Correlation of technical and biological variables with principal components (rlog transformation, n=28), Strong = significant in both PCs or |r| > 0.5 in one PC, Moderate = significant in one PC with |r| 0.3–0.5, None = not significant in either PC.
PC1 (76%)
PC2 (6%)
Variable r/F p-value Sig. r/F p-value Sig. Overall significance
Condition 0.684 0.6840 ns 0.957 0.488 ns none
Exposure batch -0.287 0.1390 ns -0.372 0.051 borderline bordeline
RIN -0.275 0.1560 ns 0.043 0.827 ns none
Duplication rate -0.501 0.0070
0.112 0.569 ns strong
Size factor 0.644 0.0002 ** 0.021 0.916 ns strong
5’-3’ bias -0.257 0.1870 ns 0.256 0.188 ns none
Proper pairs 0.448 0.0170
-0.149 0.449 ns moderate

In the female subset, PC1 was significantly driven by size factor, duplication rate, and proper pairs. Exposure replicate and RIN were not significant. PC2 showed no significant associations with any variable, with exposure replicate borderline. Experimental condition remained non-significant on both PCs. The technical gradient along PC1 persisted in the female subset.

To assess whether the technical gradient along PC1 was driven by individual extreme samples rather than being a dataset-wide characteristic, a sensitivity PCA was performed after removing the two samples with the most extreme size factors: Ctrl8F2 (SF=4.18) and Ctrl8F3 (SF=0.48, also flagged for poor QC metrics). These two samples represented opposite ends of the size factor distribution and were therefore the most likely candidates to be driving the technical gradient artificially.

# Check which samples are driving PC1
pca_f_sorted <- pca_f[order(pca_f$PC1), ]

cat("\nTop 5 PC1 (most positive):\n")
## 
## Top 5 PC1 (most positive):
print(pca_f_sorted[(nrow(pca_f_sorted)-4):nrow(pca_f_sorted), 
                    c("name", "condition", "PC1", 
                      "size_factor", "dup_rate")])
##                      name condition      PC1 size_factor dup_rate
## AcidTemp56F2 AcidTemp56F2  AcidTemp 61.73535    1.286176     47.6
## Ctrl7F3           Ctrl7F3      Ctrl 70.04500    1.441801     45.8
## Temp35F2         Temp35F2      Temp 79.31820    2.436176     41.1
## Ctrl8F2           Ctrl8F2      Ctrl 83.77702    4.178777     17.2
## Acid21F3         Acid21F3      Acid 86.09435    2.023885     32.3
cat("Bottom 5 PC1 (most negative):\n")
## Bottom 5 PC1 (most negative):
print(pca_f_sorted[1:5, c("name", "condition", "PC1", 
                            "size_factor", "dup_rate")])
##                      name condition       PC1 size_factor dup_rate
## PFOSTemp80F3 PFOSTemp80F3  PFOSTemp -50.42833   1.3245612     38.3
## PFOSTemp79F3 PFOSTemp79F3  PFOSTemp -49.56415   0.9534384     55.7
## PFOS42F3         PFOS42F3      PFOS -46.65862   0.6705504     57.2
## APT93F3           APT93F3       APT -46.49974   0.6324522     45.8
## Temp32F3         Temp32F3      Temp -45.00947   0.4470945     62.7
# Note: Ctrl8F3 is not an extreme outlier, here the most extreme samples are Ctrl8F2 and Acid21F3

# Check the different variables for the samples flagged as outliers
cat("\nQC metrics for flagged samples:\n")
## 
## QC metrics for flagged samples:
pca_f[pca_f$name %in% c("Ctrl8F3", "Ctrl8F2", 
                          "Acid18F2", "AcidPFOS67F2","Acid21F3"), 
      c("name", "condition", "PC1", "PC2",
        "size_factor", "dup_rate", "bias_5_3", 
        "proper_pairs")]
##                      name condition       PC1       PC2 size_factor dup_rate
## Acid18F2         Acid18F2      Acid -16.75018 24.125489   0.5249951     67.5
## Acid21F3         Acid21F3      Acid  86.09435 12.405187   2.0238848     32.3
## AcidPFOS67F2 AcidPFOS67F2  AcidPFOS -38.93513  4.315649   0.7389415     64.8
## Ctrl8F2           Ctrl8F2      Ctrl  83.77702 -4.823893   4.1787768     17.2
## Ctrl8F3           Ctrl8F3      Ctrl -30.47290 -3.460282   0.5457508     70.5
##              bias_5_3 proper_pairs
## Acid18F2         1.90         26.2
## Acid21F3         1.55         50.0
## AcidPFOS67F2     1.80         26.4
## Ctrl8F2          1.43         59.4
## Ctrl8F3          1.74         21.9
# Sensitivity check: what happen when the worst outliers are removed:
meta_f_clean <- meta_females[!rownames(meta_females) %in% 
                               c("Ctrl8F2", "Ctrl8F3"), ]
counts_f_clean <- counts[, rownames(meta_f_clean)]

dds_f_clean <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(
  countData = counts_f_clean,
  colData   = meta_f_clean,
  design    = ~ 1
)
## converting counts to integer mode
rld_f_clean <- rlog(dds_f_clean, blind = TRUE)

pca_f_clean <- plotPCA(rld_f_clean,
                        intgroup = c("condition", "sex"),
                        returnData = TRUE)
## using ntop=500 top features by variance
percentVar_clean <- round(100 * attr(pca_f_clean, "percentVar"))
cat("PC1:", percentVar_clean[1], "%\n")
## PC1: 80 %
cat("PC2:", percentVar_clean[2], "%\n")
## PC2: 5 %
# PC are not improved

# Check size factor correlation
pca_f_clean$size_factor <- meta_f_clean[pca_f_clean$name, "size_factor"]
cor.test(pca_f_clean$PC1, pca_f_clean$size_factor)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_f_clean$PC1 and pca_f_clean$size_factor
## t = 4.373, df = 24, p-value = 0.0002046
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  0.3754092 0.8372983
## sample estimates:
##       cor 
## 0.6659253
# size factor correlation unchanged (r = 0.67)

After removal, the size factor correlation with PC1 remained strong and essentially unchanged (r=0.67, p=0.0002 versus r=0.644 before removal), and the variance explained by PC1 did not decrease. This confirms that the technical gradient is a dataset-wide characteristic present across all samples, not an artefact of one or two extreme samples. We performed a second sensitivity check by removing all the potential outliers.

# Remove ALL flagged samples as:
# Ctrl8F3: fails 3 QC metrics
# Ctrl8F2: extreme size factor
# Acid18F2: highest 5'-3' bias, low proper pairs
# AcidPFOS67F2: elevated 5'-3' bias, low proper pairs
# Acid21F3: extreme PC1 position

meta_f_allflagged <- meta_females[
  !rownames(meta_females) %in%
    c("Ctrl8F2", "Ctrl8F3", 
      "Acid18F2", "AcidPFOS67F2", 
      "Acid21F3"), ]

counts_f_allflagged <- counts[, rownames(meta_f_allflagged)]

dds_f_allflagged <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(
  countData = counts_f_allflagged,
  colData   = meta_f_allflagged,
  design    = ~ 1)
## converting counts to integer mode
rld_f_allflagged <- rlog(dds_f_allflagged, blind = TRUE)

pca_f_allflagged <- plotPCA(rld_f_allflagged,
                             intgroup = c("condition", "sex"),
                             returnData = TRUE)
## using ntop=500 top features by variance
percentVar_allflagged <- round(
  100 * attr(pca_f_allflagged, "percentVar"))
cat("After removing all flagged samples:\n")
## After removing all flagged samples:
cat("PC1:", percentVar_allflagged[1], "%\n")
## PC1: 80 %
cat("PC2:", percentVar_allflagged[2], "%\n")
## PC2: 5 %
pca_f_allflagged$size_factor <- as.numeric(
  meta_f_allflagged[pca_f_allflagged$name, "size_factor"])

cor.test(pca_f_allflagged$PC1, pca_f_allflagged$size_factor)
## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  pca_f_allflagged$PC1 and pca_f_allflagged$size_factor
## t = 3.4048, df = 21, p-value = 0.002668
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  0.2442221 0.8095706
## sample estimates:
##       cor 
## 0.5963867
# Check remaining sample sizes per condition
cat("\nSample sizes after removing all flagged samples:\n")
## 
## Sample sizes after removing all flagged samples:
print(table(meta_f_allflagged$condition))
## 
##     Acid AcidPFOS AcidTemp      APT     Ctrl     PFOS PFOSTemp     Temp 
##        1        2        3        3        4        3        3        4

Removing all five flagged samples did not substantially reduce the technical gradient and the size factor correlation with PC1 remained strong (r=0.596, p=0.003 versus r=0.644 before removal), confirming that the technical variation is a dataset-wide characteristic inherent to the library quality across all samples rather than being driven by individual outliers. Furthermore, removing all flagged samples reduced the Acid condition to n=1 and AcidPFOS to n=2, making differential expression analysis statistically impossible for these conditions.

To inform individual exclusion decisions, the quality metrics of all flagged samples were compared to the dataset median. The full distribution of 5’-3’ bias was also examined to identify natural breaks in the data that could justify exclusion thresholds.

# Get the dataset medians as reference
cat("Dataset medians (n=28 females):\n")
## Dataset medians (n=28 females):
cat("Duplication rate:", median(meta_females$duplication_rate), "\n")
## Duplication rate: 54.75
cat("5-3 bias:", median(meta_females$five_three_prim_bias), "\n")
## 5-3 bias: 1.54
cat("Proper pairs:", median(meta_females$proper_pairs), "\n")
## Proper pairs: 35.6
# Get all the flagged samples to compare to medians
cat("\nFlagged sample metrics:\n")
## 
## Flagged sample metrics:
flagged_samples <- c("Ctrl8F2", "Ctrl8F3", 
                     "Acid18F2", "AcidPFOS67F2", "Acid21F3")

meta_females[flagged_samples,
             c("condition", "duplication_rate",
               "five_three_prim_bias", "proper_pairs")]
##              condition duplication_rate five_three_prim_bias proper_pairs
## Ctrl8F2           Ctrl             17.2                 1.43         59.4
## Ctrl8F3           Ctrl             70.5                 1.74         21.9
## Acid18F2          Acid             67.5                 1.90         26.2
## AcidPFOS67F2  AcidPFOS             64.8                 1.80         26.4
## Acid21F3          Acid             32.3                 1.55         50.0
# Full distribution of 5'-3' bias to assess outliers
cat("\nFull distribution of 5'-3' bias (sorted):\n")
## 
## Full distribution of 5'-3' bias (sorted):
sort(meta_females$five_three_prim_bias)
##  [1] 1.41 1.43 1.43 1.45 1.45 1.45 1.47 1.49 1.49 1.49 1.49 1.51 1.54 1.54 1.54
## [16] 1.54 1.55 1.55 1.55 1.55 1.56 1.62 1.63 1.67 1.69 1.74 1.80 1.90

Outlier assessment and sample exclusion decision

PCA identified substantial spread along PC1 (76%) with several samples showing extreme positions. One sample showed a notable separation on PC2 (Acid18F2, PC2=+24.1). However, PCA position alone was not used as a criterion for exclusion. All exclusion decisions were based on technical quality metrics from the MultiQC report, following a consistent rule: a sample was excluded only if it failed multiple quality metrics simultaneously and its removal did not reduce any experimental condition below n=3.Applying this rule, five samples were assessed:

Ctrl8F2 shows an extreme size factor (SF=4.18) and anomalously low duplication rate (17.2%), positioning it as a strong PCA outlier. However its 5’-3’ bias (1.43) and proper pairs (59.4%) are within acceptable range. To assess whether it was driving the technical gradient along PC1, two sensitivity PCAs were performed: first removing Ctrl8F2 and Ctrl8F3 only, then removing all five flagged samples simultaneously. In both cases the size factor correlation with PC1 remained strong and significant (r=0.67 and r=0.60 respectively), confirming that the technical gradient is a dataset-wide characteristic and not driven by this sample. Ctrl8F2 is therefore retained and its extreme library complexity is handled internally by DESeq2 size factor normalisation.

Acid21F3 shows an extreme PC1 position (+86.1) but its quality metrics are acceptable (duplication 32.3%, 5’-3’ bias 1.55, proper pairs 50.0%). Its PCA position likely reflects biological variability rather than technical failure. Acid21F3 is therefore retained.

Ctrl8F3 fails on three quality metrics simultaneously: duplication rate 70.5% (dataset median 54.75%), 5’-3’ bias 1.74 (dataset median 1.54), and proper pairs 21.9% (dataset median 35.6%). The 5’-3’ bias distribution revealed a natural break after 1.69, with Ctrl8F3 clearly separated from the main cluster. As a control sample, its removal leaves n=5 control females which is sufficient. Ctrl8F3 is excluded from all analyses.

Acid18F2 shows elevated 5’-3’ bias (1.90 — highest in the dataset) and low proper pairs (26.2%). However its removal would reduce the Acid condition to n=2 females, which is insufficient for reliable dispersion estimation in DESeq2. Acid18F2 is retained in the main analysis but excluded in the sensitivity analysis.

AcidPFOS67F2 shows elevated 5’-3’ bias (1.80) and low proper pairs (26.4%). Same reasoning as Acid18F2: removal would reduce AcidPFOS to n=2. AcidPFOS67F2 is retained in the main analysis but excluded in the sensitivity analysis.

Final sample selection

Based on the outlier assessment above, two sample sets were defined for all downstream analyses. The main analysis retained all samples except Ctrl8F3. The sensitivity analysis additionally excluded Acid18F2 and AcidPFOS67F2 to assess whether borderline samples affect the conclusions.

# Main analysis
meta_f_main <- meta_females[
  rownames(meta_females) != "Ctrl8F3", ]
counts_f_main <- counts[, rownames(meta_f_main)]

# Sensitivity analysis: females, all three flagged samples removed
meta_f_sensitivity <- meta_females[
  !rownames(meta_females) %in% 
    c("Ctrl8F3", "Acid18F2", "AcidPFOS67F2"), ]
counts_f_sensitivity <- counts[, rownames(meta_f_sensitivity)]

# Check sample distributions
cat("MAIN n =", nrow(meta_f_main), "\n")
## MAIN n = 27
print(table(meta_f_main$condition, meta_f_main$exposure))
##           
##            2 3
##   Acid     1 2
##   AcidPFOS 2 1
##   AcidTemp 2 1
##   APT      1 2
##   Ctrl     3 2
##   PFOS     1 2
##   PFOSTemp 0 3
##   Temp     2 2
cat("SENSITIVITY n =", nrow(meta_f_sensitivity), "\n")
## SENSITIVITY n = 25
print(table(meta_f_sensitivity$condition, meta_f_sensitivity$exposure))
##           
##            2 3
##   Acid     0 2
##   AcidPFOS 1 1
##   AcidTemp 2 1
##   APT      1 2
##   Ctrl     3 2
##   PFOS     1 2
##   PFOSTemp 0 3
##   Temp     2 2

DESeq2 - main analysis

The DESeq2 model includes the exposure batch as a covariate and the exposure conditions as the variable of interest (~ exposure + condition). Including the exposure batch accounts for systematic differences between the two independent biological replicate experiments. For the condition comparison, control is the reference group for all pairwise comparisons. Genes with very low counts were removed before fitting the model. We retained only the genes with at least 10 counts in a minimum of 3 samples. This pre-filtering step improves statistical power by reducing the number of tests submitted to multiple testing correction.

# Set control as reference
meta_f_main$condition <- relevel(
  factor(meta_f_main$condition), ref = "Ctrl")
meta_f_main$exposure  <- factor(meta_f_main$exposure)

# Build the DESeq2 object
dds_main_f <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(countData = counts_f_main,
                              colData = meta_f_main,
                              design= ~ exposure + condition)
## converting counts to integer mode
# Filter genes with low count 
# Keep genes with >= 10 counts in at least 3 samples
keep <- rowSums(counts(dds_main_f) >= 10) >= 3
dds_main_f <- dds_main_f[keep, ]

cat("Genes before filtering:", nrow(counts_f_main), "\n")
## Genes before filtering: 32520
cat("Genes after filtering:", nrow(dds_main_f), "\n")
## Genes after filtering: 15989
# 32520 -> 15989 genes retained (49%) — standard for liver RNA-seq

Run the DESEQ model

dds_main_f <- DESeq(dds_main_f)
## estimating size factors
## estimating dispersions
## gene-wise dispersion estimates
## mean-dispersion relationship
## final dispersion estimates
## fitting model and testing
# Dispersion estimates diagnostic plot
plotDispEsts(dds_main_f,
             main = "Dispersion estimates females primary analysis")

# Cook's distance 
boxplot(log10(assays(dds_main_f)[["cooks"]]),
        range = 0,
        las = 2,
        main = "Cook's distance per sample",
        ylab = "log10(Cook's distance)",
        col = "lightblue")

# Check model terms
cat("\nModel terms:\n")
## 
## Model terms:
print(resultsNames(dds_main_f))
## [1] "Intercept"                  "exposure_3_vs_2"           
## [3] "condition_Acid_vs_Ctrl"     "condition_AcidPFOS_vs_Ctrl"
## [5] "condition_AcidTemp_vs_Ctrl" "condition_APT_vs_Ctrl"     
## [7] "condition_PFOS_vs_Ctrl"     "condition_PFOSTemp_vs_Ctrl"
## [9] "condition_Temp_vs_Ctrl"

Cook’s distance per sample showed comparable distributions across all retained samples, indicating that no single sample exerts disproportionate influence on the differential expression results across the transcriptome.

Comparisons betwen each condition and control

Pairwise Wald tests were performed for each condition against Control with independent filtering: DESeq2 automatically finds the optimal count threshold per comparison that maximizes the number of genes passing multiple testing correction. The Cook’s distance cutoff was also enabled (setting adjusted p-values to NA for genes with extreme outlier counts in individual samples). P-adjusted p-values are obtained after applying Benjamini-Hochberg correction.

# Check comparisons by extracting Wald test results for each condition vs. Control
res_Acid <- results(dds_main_f,
                    contrast = c("condition", "Acid", "Ctrl"),
                    independentFiltering = TRUE,
                    cooksCutoff = TRUE)
res_Temp    <- results(dds_main_f,
                       contrast = c("condition", "Temp", "Ctrl"),
                       independentFiltering = TRUE,
                       cooksCutoff = TRUE)

res_PFOS    <- results(dds_main_f,
                       contrast = c("condition", "PFOS", "Ctrl"),
                       independentFiltering = TRUE,
                       cooksCutoff = TRUE)

res_AcidTemp <- results(dds_main_f,
                        contrast = c("condition", "AcidTemp", "Ctrl"),
                        independentFiltering = TRUE,
                        cooksCutoff = TRUE)

res_PFOSTemp <- results(dds_main_f,
                        contrast = c("condition", "PFOSTemp", "Ctrl"),
                        independentFiltering = TRUE,
                        cooksCutoff = TRUE)

res_AcidPFOS <- results(dds_main_f,
                        contrast = c("condition", "AcidPFOS", "Ctrl"),
                        independentFiltering = TRUE,
                        cooksCutoff = TRUE)

res_APT     <- results(dds_main_f,
                       contrast = c("condition", "APT", "Ctrl"),
                       independentFiltering = TRUE,
                       cooksCutoff = TRUE)

# Summaries of results at p.adj < 0.05
summary(res_Acid, alpha = 0.05)
## 
## out of 15989 with nonzero total read count
## adjusted p-value < 0.05
## LFC > 0 (up)       : 2, 0.013%
## LFC < 0 (down)     : 3, 0.019%
## outliers [1]       : 184, 1.2%
## low counts [2]     : 0, 0%
## (mean count < 1)
## [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
## [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results
summary(res_Temp, alpha = 0.05)
## 
## out of 15989 with nonzero total read count
## adjusted p-value < 0.05
## LFC > 0 (up)       : 3, 0.019%
## LFC < 0 (down)     : 1, 0.0063%
## outliers [1]       : 184, 1.2%
## low counts [2]     : 0, 0%
## (mean count < 1)
## [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
## [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results
summary(res_PFOS, alpha = 0.05)
## 
## out of 15989 with nonzero total read count
## adjusted p-value < 0.05
## LFC > 0 (up)       : 16, 0.1%
## LFC < 0 (down)     : 46, 0.29%
## outliers [1]       : 184, 1.2%
## low counts [2]     : 606, 3.8%
## (mean count < 3)
## [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
## [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results
summary(res_AcidTemp, alpha = 0.05)
## 
## out of 15989 with nonzero total read count
## adjusted p-value < 0.05
## LFC > 0 (up)       : 48, 0.3%
## LFC < 0 (down)     : 78, 0.49%
## outliers [1]       : 184, 1.2%
## low counts [2]     : 0, 0%
## (mean count < 1)
## [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
## [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results
summary(res_PFOSTemp, alpha = 0.05)
## 
## out of 15989 with nonzero total read count
## adjusted p-value < 0.05
## LFC > 0 (up)       : 741, 4.6%
## LFC < 0 (down)     : 1481, 9.3%
## outliers [1]       : 184, 1.2%
## low counts [2]     : 606, 3.8%
## (mean count < 3)
## [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
## [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results
summary(res_AcidPFOS, alpha = 0.05)
## 
## out of 15989 with nonzero total read count
## adjusted p-value < 0.05
## LFC > 0 (up)       : 37, 0.23%
## LFC < 0 (down)     : 38, 0.24%
## outliers [1]       : 184, 1.2%
## low counts [2]     : 606, 3.8%
## (mean count < 3)
## [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
## [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results
summary(res_APT, alpha = 0.05)
## 
## out of 15989 with nonzero total read count
## adjusted p-value < 0.05
## LFC > 0 (up)       : 38, 0.24%
## LFC < 0 (down)     : 59, 0.37%
## outliers [1]       : 184, 1.2%
## low counts [2]     : 2126, 13%
## (mean count < 6)
## [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
## [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results

The 184 outlier genes detected in all comparisons reflect Cook’s distance filtering applied at the model level. These genes received NA adjusted p-values and were excluded from multiple testing correction.

LFC shrinkage

LFC shrinkage was applied using the ashr method (Stephens 2017) to obtain reliable log2 fold change estimates. This reduces noisy fold change estimates from lowly expressed genes toward zero while leaving well-estimated fold changes from highly expressed genes unchanged. The shrinkage does not affect the statistical significance of results (padj values are not modified) but improves the reliability of fold change estimates used for filtering and visualization. This is important for datasets with small n per condition and high variability. The ashr method was selected over apeglm because it supports contrast-based comparisons between experimental conditions (like combination vs single stressor), while apeglm only allows coefficient based comparisons against the reference.

# LFC shrinkage with ashr
resLFC_Acid <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
                          contrast = c("condition", "Acid", "Ctrl"),
                          type = "ashr")
resLFC_Temp <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
                          contrast = c("condition", "Temp", "Ctrl"),
                          type = "ashr")
resLFC_PFOS <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
                          contrast = c("condition", "PFOS", "Ctrl"),
                          type = "ashr")
resLFC_AcidTemp <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
                               contrast = c("condition", "AcidTemp", "Ctrl"),
                               type = "ashr")
resLFC_PFOSTemp <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
                               contrast = c("condition", "PFOSTemp", "Ctrl"),
                               type = "ashr")
resLFC_AcidPFOS <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
                               contrast = c("condition", "AcidPFOS", "Ctrl"),
                               type = "ashr")
resLFC_APT <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
                         contrast = c("condition", "APT", "Ctrl"),
                         type = "ashr")

# Verify all summaries match previous results
summary(resLFC_Acid, alpha = 0.05)
## 
## out of 15989 with nonzero total read count
## adjusted p-value < 0.05
## LFC > 0 (up)       : 2, 0.013%
## LFC < 0 (down)     : 3, 0.019%
## outliers [1]       : 184, 1.2%
## low counts [2]     : 0, 0%
## (mean count < 1)
## [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
## [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results
summary(resLFC_Temp, alpha = 0.05)
## 
## out of 15989 with nonzero total read count
## adjusted p-value < 0.05
## LFC > 0 (up)       : 3, 0.019%
## LFC < 0 (down)     : 1, 0.0063%
## outliers [1]       : 184, 1.2%
## low counts [2]     : 0, 0%
## (mean count < 1)
## [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
## [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results
summary(resLFC_PFOS, alpha = 0.05)
## 
## out of 15989 with nonzero total read count
## adjusted p-value < 0.05
## LFC > 0 (up)       : 16, 0.1%
## LFC < 0 (down)     : 46, 0.29%
## outliers [1]       : 184, 1.2%
## low counts [2]     : 606, 3.8%
## (mean count < 3)
## [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
## [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results
summary(resLFC_AcidTemp, alpha = 0.05)
## 
## out of 15989 with nonzero total read count
## adjusted p-value < 0.05
## LFC > 0 (up)       : 48, 0.3%
## LFC < 0 (down)     : 78, 0.49%
## outliers [1]       : 184, 1.2%
## low counts [2]     : 0, 0%
## (mean count < 1)
## [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
## [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results
summary(resLFC_PFOSTemp, alpha = 0.05)
## 
## out of 15989 with nonzero total read count
## adjusted p-value < 0.05
## LFC > 0 (up)       : 741, 4.6%
## LFC < 0 (down)     : 1481, 9.3%
## outliers [1]       : 184, 1.2%
## low counts [2]     : 606, 3.8%
## (mean count < 3)
## [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
## [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results
summary(resLFC_AcidPFOS, alpha = 0.05)
## 
## out of 15989 with nonzero total read count
## adjusted p-value < 0.05
## LFC > 0 (up)       : 37, 0.23%
## LFC < 0 (down)     : 38, 0.24%
## outliers [1]       : 184, 1.2%
## low counts [2]     : 606, 3.8%
## (mean count < 3)
## [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
## [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results
summary(resLFC_APT, alpha = 0.05)
## 
## out of 15989 with nonzero total read count
## adjusted p-value < 0.05
## LFC > 0 (up)       : 38, 0.24%
## LFC < 0 (down)     : 59, 0.37%
## outliers [1]       : 184, 1.2%
## low counts [2]     : 2126, 13%
## (mean count < 6)
## [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
## [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results

Get significant genes

Genes were considered differentially expressed if the adjusted p-value < 0.05 after Benjamini-Hochberg correction, and the log2 fold change ≥ log2(1.5), corresponding to a minimum 1.5-fold change in either direction. The log2(1.5) threshold was chosen as a biologically meaningful minimum effect size given the high within-group variability in this dataset. All results use shrunken LFC estimates from the previous step.

# Get genes names
gene_annotations <- data.frame(
  gene_id   = rownames(counts),
  gene_name = genes_names,
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
rownames(gene_annotations) <- gene_annotations$gene_id

# Check the annotation table looks correct
head(gene_annotations)
##                               gene_id gene_name
## ENSDARG00000101324 ENSDARG00000101324    apoa1b
## ENSDARG00000063905 ENSDARG00000063905    mt-co1
## ENSDARG00000015866 ENSDARG00000015866     apoa2
## ENSDARG00000038439 ENSDARG00000038439   fabp10a
## ENSDARG00000092170 ENSDARG00000092170     apoc1
## ENSDARG00000063908 ENSDARG00000063908    mt-co2
# Create a function to get significant gene in each comparison
get_sig_genes <- function(res, condition_name,
                           padj_thresh = 0.05,
                           lfc_thresh = log2(1.5)) {
  
  res_df <- as.data.frame(res)
  res_df$gene_id   <- rownames(res_df)
  res_df$gene_name <- gene_annotations[rownames(res_df), "gene_name"]
  res_df$condition <- condition_name
  
  # Classify each gene
  res_df$direction <- "NS"  # default
  res_df$direction[is.na(res_df$padj)] <- "NA"
  res_df$direction[!is.na(res_df$padj) & 
                 res_df$padj < padj_thresh & 
                 res_df$log2FoldChange >= lfc_thresh] <- "UP"
  res_df$direction[!is.na(res_df$padj) & 
                 res_df$padj < padj_thresh & 
                 res_df$log2FoldChange <= -lfc_thresh] <- "DOWN"
  
  res_df[order(res_df$padj, na.last = TRUE), ]
}

# Apply to all vs Control comparisons
results_Acid     <- get_sig_genes(resLFC_Acid,     "Acid")
results_Temp     <- get_sig_genes(resLFC_Temp,     "Temp")
results_PFOS     <- get_sig_genes(resLFC_PFOS,     "PFOS")
results_AcidTemp <- get_sig_genes(resLFC_AcidTemp, "AcidTemp")
results_PFOSTemp <- get_sig_genes(resLFC_PFOSTemp, "PFOSTemp")
results_AcidPFOS <- get_sig_genes(resLFC_AcidPFOS, "AcidPFOS")
results_APT      <- get_sig_genes(resLFC_APT,      "APT")

# DEG summary
deg_summary <- sapply(
  list(Acid     = results_Acid,
       Temp     = results_Temp,
       PFOS     = results_PFOS,
       AcidTemp = results_AcidTemp,
       PFOSTemp = results_PFOSTemp,
       AcidPFOS = results_AcidPFOS,
       APT      = results_APT),
  function(r) c(
    UP    = sum(r$direction == "UP"),
    DOWN  = sum(r$direction == "DOWN"),
    TOTAL = sum(r$direction %in% c("UP", "DOWN"))
  )
)

cat("DEG summary (padj < 0.05 AND |log2FC| >= log2(1.5)):\n")
## DEG summary (padj < 0.05 AND |log2FC| >= log2(1.5)):
print(t(deg_summary))
##           UP DOWN TOTAL
## Acid       2    3     5
## Temp       3    1     4
## PFOS      16   46    62
## AcidTemp  48   77   125
## PFOSTemp 739 1481  2220
## AcidPFOS  36   38    74
## APT       37   59    96

LFC shrinkage single vs. multiple stressor

To determine whether each combination treatment produces a response beyond what any individual stressor alone would predict, pairwise comparisons were made between each combination and its single stressors. For example, AcidTemp was compared to both Acid alone and Temp alone. A low DEG count in a comparison means the two conditions have similar transcriptional profiles = the combination is similar to the single stressor. A high DEG count means the combination is different from the stressor alone.

These comparisons used the same ashr LFC shrinkage and the same significance thresholds as the vs-Control comparisons (padj < 0.05, |log2FC| ≥ log2(1.5)).

# Single vs combination comparisons

#AcidTemp vs. Acid and Temp
resLFC_AcidTemp_vs_Acid <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
  contrast = c("condition", "AcidTemp", "Acid"), 
  type = "ashr")

resLFC_AcidTemp_vs_Temp <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
  contrast = c("condition", "AcidTemp", "Temp"), 
  type = "ashr")

#PFOSTemp vs. PFOS and Temp
resLFC_PFOSTemp_vs_PFOS <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
  contrast = c("condition", "PFOSTemp", "PFOS"), 
  type = "ashr")

resLFC_PFOSTemp_vs_Temp <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
  contrast = c("condition", "PFOSTemp", "Temp"), 
  type = "ashr")

#AcidPFOS vs. Acid and PFOS
resLFC_AcidPFOS_vs_Acid <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
  contrast = c("condition", "AcidPFOS", "Acid"), 
  type = "ashr")

resLFC_AcidPFOS_vs_PFOS <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
  contrast = c("condition", "AcidPFOS", "PFOS"), 
  type = "ashr")

#APT vs. Acid, Temp and PFOS
resLFC_APT_vs_Acid <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
  contrast = c("condition", "APT", "Acid"), 
  type = "ashr")

resLFC_APT_vs_Temp <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
  contrast = c("condition", "APT", "Temp"), 
  type = "ashr")

resLFC_APT_vs_PFOS <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
  contrast = c("condition", "APT", "PFOS"), 
  type = "ashr")

#APT vs. double stressors
resLFC_APT_vs_AcidTemp <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
  contrast = c("condition", "APT", "AcidTemp"), 
  type = "ashr")

resLFC_APT_vs_PFOSTemp <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
  contrast = c("condition", "APT", "PFOSTemp"), 
  type = "ashr")

resLFC_APT_vs_AcidPFOS <- lfcShrink(dds_main_f,
  contrast = c("condition", "APT", "AcidPFOS"), 
  type = "ashr")

# Apply get_sig_genes to all comparisons
results_AcidTemp_vs_Acid <- get_sig_genes(
  resLFC_AcidTemp_vs_Acid, "AcidTemp_vs_Acid")
results_AcidTemp_vs_Temp <- get_sig_genes(
  resLFC_AcidTemp_vs_Temp, "AcidTemp_vs_Temp")
results_PFOSTemp_vs_PFOS <- get_sig_genes(
  resLFC_PFOSTemp_vs_PFOS, "PFOSTemp_vs_PFOS")
results_PFOSTemp_vs_Temp <- get_sig_genes(
  resLFC_PFOSTemp_vs_Temp, "PFOSTemp_vs_Temp")
results_AcidPFOS_vs_Acid <- get_sig_genes(
  resLFC_AcidPFOS_vs_Acid, "AcidPFOS_vs_Acid")
results_AcidPFOS_vs_PFOS <- get_sig_genes(
  resLFC_AcidPFOS_vs_PFOS, "AcidPFOS_vs_PFOS")
results_APT_vs_Acid <- get_sig_genes(
  resLFC_APT_vs_Acid, "APT_vs_Acid")
results_APT_vs_Temp <- get_sig_genes(
  resLFC_APT_vs_Temp, "APT_vs_Temp")
results_APT_vs_PFOS <- get_sig_genes(
  resLFC_APT_vs_PFOS, "APT_vs_PFOS")
results_APT_vs_AcidTemp <- get_sig_genes(
  resLFC_APT_vs_AcidTemp, "APT_vs_AcidTemp")
results_APT_vs_PFOSTemp <- get_sig_genes(
  resLFC_APT_vs_PFOSTemp, "APT_vs_PFOSTemp")
results_APT_vs_AcidPFOS <- get_sig_genes(
  resLFC_APT_vs_AcidPFOS, "APT_vs_AcidPFOS")

# DEG count summary 
deg_summary_combinations <- sapply(
  list(AcidTemp_vs_Acid   = results_AcidTemp_vs_Acid,
       AcidTemp_vs_Temp   = results_AcidTemp_vs_Temp,
       PFOSTemp_vs_PFOS   = results_PFOSTemp_vs_PFOS,
       PFOSTemp_vs_Temp   = results_PFOSTemp_vs_Temp,
       AcidPFOS_vs_Acid   = results_AcidPFOS_vs_Acid,
       AcidPFOS_vs_PFOS   = results_AcidPFOS_vs_PFOS,
       APT_vs_Acid        = results_APT_vs_Acid,
       APT_vs_Temp        = results_APT_vs_Temp,
       APT_vs_PFOS        = results_APT_vs_PFOS,
       APT_vs_AcidTemp    = results_APT_vs_AcidTemp,
       APT_vs_PFOSTemp    = results_APT_vs_PFOSTemp,
       APT_vs_AcidPFOS    = results_APT_vs_AcidPFOS),
  function(r) c(
    UP    = sum(r$direction == "UP"),
    DOWN  = sum(r$direction == "DOWN"),
    TOTAL = sum(r$direction %in% c("UP","DOWN"))
  )
)

cat("DEG summary — single vs combination comparisons:\n")
## DEG summary — single vs combination comparisons:
print(t(deg_summary_combinations))
##                   UP DOWN TOTAL
## AcidTemp_vs_Acid  51  149   200
## AcidTemp_vs_Temp   6   24    30
## PFOSTemp_vs_PFOS 287  474   761
## PFOSTemp_vs_Temp 699 1080  1779
## AcidPFOS_vs_Acid  27   72    99
## AcidPFOS_vs_PFOS  20   25    45
## APT_vs_Acid       32   65    97
## APT_vs_Temp        3    5     8
## APT_vs_PFOS       18   12    30
## APT_vs_AcidTemp   14    4    18
## APT_vs_PFOSTemp  730  792  1522
## APT_vs_AcidPFOS    7    3    10

The number of DEGs in each comparison reveals which stressor dominates each combination response and whether any combination produces a synergistic transcriptional profile.

Interpretation:

AcidTemp vs single stressors: AcidTemp vs Acid: 200 DEGs –> AcidTemp is substantially different from Acid alone AcidTemp vs Temp: only 30 DEGs –> AcidTemp is very similar to Temp alone Interpretation: Temperature drives the AcidTemp response, acid adds little

PFOSTemp vs single stressors: PFOSTemp vs PFOS: 761 DEGs –> very different from PFOS alone PFOSTemp vs Temp: 1779 DEGs –> very different from Temp alone Interpretation: PFOSTemp is synergistic, it is not just one stressor driving the response

AcidPFOS vs single stressors: AcidPFOS vs Acid: 99 DEGs AcidPFOS vs PFOS: only 45 DEGs –> AcidPFOS is similar to PFOS alone Interpretation: PFOS drives the AcidPFOS response, acid adds a little

APT vs combinations: APT vs PFOSTemp: 1522 DEGs –> APT is dramatically different from PFOSTemp APT vs AcidPFOS: only 10 DEGs –> APT is almost identical to AcidPFOS APT vs AcidTemp: only 18 DEGs –> APT is almost identical to AcidTemp Interpretation: Adding the third stressor to PFOSTemp completely changes the response. It seems that adding acid to PFOSTemp dampens the response.

P-value distribution diagnostics

P-value histograms can be used as diagnostic for assessing whether the statistical model is behaving correctly for each comparison. Under a well-specified model, genes with no real difference between conditions produce uniformly distributed p-values between 0 and 1. Genes with real differential expression produce an enrichment of small p-values near zero on top of this uniform background. Four patterns are possible: * A spike near zero with a flat background = good signal –> real DEGs exist and the model is well specified. * A perfectly flat distribution = true null result (no differential expression) or underpowered comparison. * A spike near 1 instead of near 0 = model misspecification –> confounding variable not accounted for in the design formula or high within-group variance relative to between-group differences. * An irregular or bimodal distribution = more serious problem needing investigation

Used to know if the signal is real or an issue with samples statistical power

par(mfrow = c(2,4))
for(nm in c("Acid","Temp","PFOS","AcidTemp",
            "PFOSTemp","AcidPFOS","APT")) {
  res <- get(paste0("results_", nm))
  hist(res$pvalue, breaks = 50,
       main = nm, xlab = "p-value",
       col = "lightblue")
}
par(mfrow = c(1,1))

Acid and Temp have anti-conservative distributions with p-values enriched near 1 rather than near 0. This suggests that the model cannot reliably detect differential expression for these conditions, either due to insufficient statistical power or high within-group variability relative to between-group differences. All other conditions have good p-value distributions with varying degrees of enrichment near zero, consistent with real differential expression signals.

Within-group variance assessement

To understand why Acid and Temp show anti-conservative p-value distributions, we examine the within-group biological variability for each condition. DESeq2 estimates a shared dispersion across conditions so if some conditions have higher within-group variance than others, the model have difficulties to distinguish biological signal from noise for those specific comparisons. The within-group variance is calculated using DESeq2-normalised counts, which removes library size differences between samples before computing variance. The median variance across all genes is used as a summary statistic per condition.

norm_counts <- counts(dds_main_f, normalized = TRUE)
 
# Mean and variance per condition
 for(cond in levels(meta_f_main$condition)) {
     samples <- rownames(meta_f_main)[meta_f_main$condition == cond]
     cat(cond, "— n =", length(samples), 
         "| median variance:", 
         round(median(apply(norm_counts[, samples], 1, var))), "\n")
}
## Ctrl — n = 5 | median variance: 1963 
## Acid — n = 3 | median variance: 1894 
## AcidPFOS — n = 3 | median variance: 566 
## AcidTemp — n = 3 | median variance: 567 
## APT — n = 3 | median variance: 488 
## PFOS — n = 3 | median variance: 666 
## PFOSTemp — n = 3 | median variance: 152 
## Temp — n = 4 | median variance: 1449

Within-group variance differs across conditions. Ctrl, Acid and Temp show higher variance than PFOSTemp, AcidTemp and APT. Several factors contribute to this pattern.

During the developmental exposure phase (0-28 dpf), each condition within each exposure replicate was maintained in a single tank. Fish were subsequently transferred to clean water rearing conditions from 32 dpf onward and maintained until sampling at 15-17 months, during which they were distributed across one to three rearing tanks depending on survival numbers. The transcriptomic analysis therefore reflects the lasting molecular consequences of early developmental exposure rather than acute responses to current tank conditions, which reduces but does not eliminate concerns about tank-level pseudoreplication during the exposure phase.

Conditions with samples from both exposure replicates, including Ctrl, Acid, Temp and PFOS, contain fish from two independent experiments with different breeding groups, different exposure tanks, and approximately one month age difference at sampling. This introduces additional biological variability from genetic background differences and minor environmental variation between replicate experiments. PFOSTemp samples derive exclusively from exposure replicate 3, meaning all fish share the same age, breeding group and exposure tank history. This homogeneity likely contributes to the lower within-group variance observed in PFOSTemp and should be considered when interpreting the consistency of its transcriptional response.

The exposure replicate covariate in the DESeq2 model accounts for systematic mean differences between replicate experiments but does not correct for tank-level effects or differences in biological variability between groups. The practical consequence is reduced statistical power for conditions with higher variance, particularly Acid and Temp. Detected DEGs in these conditions remain statistically valid but real effects may have been missed. GSEA on the full ranked gene list is therefore used as the primary analysis for Acid and Temp as pathway-level analysis is more robust to high individual variability than single-gene testing.

Volcano plots

Volcano plots visualize the relationship between statistical significance and effect size for all tested genes simultaneously. Each point represents one gene. The x-axis shows the shrunken log2 fold change with positive values indicating higher expression and negative values indicating lower expression in teh treatment compared to control. The y-axis shows -log10(adjusted p-value): higher points are more statistically significant. Dashed vertical lines mark the log2(1.5) fold change threshold and the dashed horizontal line marks padj = 0.05. The top 15 most significant DEGs are labelled by gene name. Genes with NA adjusted p-values (Cook’s distance outliers and independently filtered genes) are not shown on the plot.

library(ggrepel)

make_volcano <- function(res_df, title,
                         padj_thresh = 0.05,
                         lfc_thresh  = log2(1.5))
                         {
# Assign colour based on the direction of expression change  
  res_df$color <- factor(
    ifelse(res_df$direction == "UP",   "Up",
    ifelse(res_df$direction == "DOWN", "Down", "NS")),
    levels = c("Up", "Down", "NS"))
  
# Label only genes with known names
  labels <- res_df[res_df$direction %in% c("UP","DOWN") &
                       !grepl("^ENSDARG", res_df$gene_name) &
                       !grepl("^si:", res_df$gene_name) &
                       !grepl("^zgc:", res_df$gene_name) &
                       !is.na(res_df$gene_name), ]
# Take only the most significant ones
  top_genes <- head(labels[order(labels$padj), ], 10)
  
  ggplot(res_df, aes(x = log2FoldChange,
                     y = -log10(padj),
                     color = color)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 0.7) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c("Up"   = "#D85A30",
                                   "Down" = "#378ADD",
                                   "NS"   = "#BBBBBB")) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = c(-lfc_thresh, lfc_thresh),
               linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.4) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(padj_thresh),
               linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.4) +
    geom_text_repel(data        = top_genes,
                    aes(label   = gene_name),
                    size        = 3,
                    max.overlaps = Inf,
                    min.segment.length = 0,
                    box.padding = 0.5,
                    point.padding = 0.3,
                    segment.color = "grey50",
                    segment.size = 0.3,
                    color = "black",
                    fontface = "italic") +
    labs(title  = title,
         x      = "log2 fold change",
         y      = "-log10(adjusted p-value)",
         color  = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
    theme(plot.title    = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
          legend.position = "bottom",
          axis.title = element_text(size = 9))
}

# Generate all volcano plots vs Ctrl
p_volc_Acid     <- make_volcano(results_Acid,     "Acid vs Ctrl")
p_volc_Temp     <- make_volcano(results_Temp,     "Temp vs Ctrl")
p_volc_PFOS     <- make_volcano(results_PFOS,     "PFOS vs Ctrl")
p_volc_AcidTemp <- make_volcano(results_AcidTemp, "AcidTemp vs Ctrl")
p_volc_PFOSTemp <- make_volcano(results_PFOSTemp, "PFOSTemp vs Ctrl")
p_volc_AcidPFOS <- make_volcano(results_AcidPFOS, "AcidPFOS vs Ctrl")
p_volc_APT      <- make_volcano(results_APT,      "APT vs Ctrl")

p_volc_AcidTemp <- p_volc_AcidTemp + coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-25, 25), ylim = c(0, 20))
p_volc_PFOSTemp <- p_volc_PFOSTemp + coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-25, 25), ylim = c(0, 20))
p_volc_AcidPFOS <- p_volc_AcidPFOS + coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-25, 25), ylim = c(0, 20))
p_volc_APT      <- p_volc_APT      + coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-25, 25), ylim = c(0, 20))

p_volc_Acid

p_volc_Temp

p_volc_PFOS

p_volc_AcidTemp

p_volc_PFOSTemp

p_volc_AcidPFOS

p_volc_APT

combined_volcano <- (p_volc_AcidTemp + p_volc_PFOSTemp) /
                 (p_volc_AcidPFOS + p_volc_APT) +
  plot_layout(guides = "collect") &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
combined_volcano

Volcano plots confirm the pattern observed in the DEG summary table. PFOSTemp shows the strongest response with 2220 DEGs, predominantly downregulated, with log2 fold changes extending beyond -20 for the most strongly suppressed genes. AcidTemp and AcidPFOS show moderate responses also dominated by downregulation. Acid and Temp show almost no significant genes, consistent with the high within-group variability identified above. Several genes appear as significant across multiple conditions. The gene zgc:123284 (predicted oxidoreductase) is upregulated in Acid, Temp and APT, suggesting a consistent response to stressor combinations independent of the specific stressor involved. In APT, the most prominent significant genes include per1a, per1b, cry1b, nfil3-5, core components of the circadian clock. This suggests that the triple stressor combination specifically disrupts circadian rhythm regulation in the liver. cry1b also appears in PFOS and PFOSTemp, indicating that circadian disruption may be partly driven by the PFOS component of the response. Gene-level interpretation is limited by the small sample sizes per condition. Pathway-level analysis using GSEA is presented in the following section to provide a more robust biological interpretation.

Stressors interaction analysis
