To identify which stressor drives each combination response and to detect emergent, non-additive transcriptomic profiles, the number of differentially expressed genes (DEGs, padj < 0.05, |log2FC| ≥ log2(1.5)) was computed for each combination versus its constituent single stressors, and for APT versus each double stressor. A low DEG count between a combination and one of its singles indicates that the single stressor dominates the combination response, while high DEG counts against both singles indicate an emergent, non-additive signature. The triple stressor APT was compared against each double combination to identify which constituents most strongly modify the APT response.

# Count DEGs for each combination vs its single stressors
# These numbers are used in Table 2 of the manuscript
count_degs <- function(res, padj_cutoff = 0.05, lfc_cutoff = log2(1.5)) {
  df <- as.data.frame(res)
  df <- df[!is.na(df$padj), ]
  up   <- sum(df$padj < padj_cutoff & df$log2FoldChange >=  lfc_cutoff)
  down <- sum(df$padj < padj_cutoff & df$log2FoldChange <= -lfc_cutoff)
  total <- up + down
  c(up = up, down = down, total = total)
}

comparisons <- list(
  list(combo = "AcidTemp", reference = "Acid", res = resLFC_AcidTemp_vs_Acid),
  list(combo = "AcidTemp", reference = "Temp", res = resLFC_AcidTemp_vs_Temp),
  list(combo = "AcidPFOS", reference = "Acid", res = resLFC_AcidPFOS_vs_Acid),
  list(combo = "AcidPFOS", reference = "PFOS", res = resLFC_AcidPFOS_vs_PFOS),
  list(combo = "PFOSTemp", reference = "PFOS", res = resLFC_PFOSTemp_vs_PFOS),
  list(combo = "PFOSTemp", reference = "Temp", res = resLFC_PFOSTemp_vs_Temp),
  list(combo = "APT", reference = "AcidTemp", res = resLFC_APT_vs_AcidTemp),
  list(combo = "APT", reference = "AcidPFOS", res = resLFC_APT_vs_AcidPFOS),
  list(combo = "APT", reference = "PFOSTemp", res = resLFC_APT_vs_PFOSTemp))

table2_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(comparisons, function(x) {
  counts <- count_degs(x$res)
  data.frame(combo = x$combo, reference = x$reference,
             up = counts["up"], down = counts["down"], total = counts["total"],
             stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
}))

cat("DEG counts (combination vs reference stressor) for Table 2:\n")
## DEG counts (combination vs reference stressor) for Table 2:
print(table2_data)
##        combo reference  up down total
## up  AcidTemp      Acid  51  149   200
## up1 AcidTemp      Temp   6   24    30
## up2 AcidPFOS      Acid  27   72    99
## up3 AcidPFOS      PFOS  20   25    45
## up4 PFOSTemp      PFOS 287  474   761
## up5 PFOSTemp      Temp 699 1080  1779
## up6      APT  AcidTemp  14    4    18
## up7      APT  AcidPFOS   7    3    10
## up8      APT  PFOSTemp 730  792  1522

Gene Set Enrichment Analysis

library(clusterProfiler)
library(org.Dr.eg.db)

# Function to convert gene IDs to ENTREZ and run gseKEGG
run_kegg_gsea <- function(res, condition_name) {
  set.seed(42)
  
  df <- as.data.frame(res)
  df$gene_id <- rownames(df)
  df <- df[!is.na(df$log2FoldChange), ]
  
  # Convert Ensembl to ENTREZ
  entrez_map <- mapIds(
    org.Dr.eg.db,
    keys = df$gene_id,
    column = "ENTREZID",
    keytype = "ENSEMBL",
    multiVals = "first")
  
  df$entrez <- entrez_map
  df <- df[!is.na(df$entrez), ]
  df <- df[!duplicated(df$entrez), ]
  
  # Build ranked vector
  ranked <- df$log2FoldChange
  names(ranked) <- df$entrez
  ranked <- sort(ranked, decreasing = TRUE)
  
  # Run gseKEGG
  res_kegg <- tryCatch(
    gseKEGG(
      geneList = ranked,
      organism = "dre",   # zebrafish
      pvalueCutoff = 1,
      verbose = FALSE),
    error = function(e) NULL)

  # Filter to pathways with q-value < 0.25 (Subramanian threshold)
  if (!is.null(res_kegg) && nrow(res_kegg) > 0) {
    res_kegg@result <- res_kegg@result[res_kegg@result$qvalue < 0.25, ]
  }
  
  return(res_kegg)
}  

# Run on all 7 conditions
kegg_list <- list(
  Acid     = run_kegg_gsea(resLFC_Acid,     "Acid"),
  Temp     = run_kegg_gsea(resLFC_Temp,     "Temp"),
  PFOS     = run_kegg_gsea(resLFC_PFOS,     "PFOS"),
  AcidTemp = run_kegg_gsea(resLFC_AcidTemp, "AcidTemp"),
  AcidPFOS = run_kegg_gsea(resLFC_AcidPFOS, "AcidPFOS"),
  PFOSTemp = run_kegg_gsea(resLFC_PFOSTemp, "PFOSTemp"),
  APT      = run_kegg_gsea(resLFC_APT,      "APT"))
## 'select()' returned 1:many mapping between keys and columns
## Reading KEGG annotation online: "https://rest.kegg.jp/link/dre/pathway"...
## Reading KEGG annotation online: "https://rest.kegg.jp/list/pathway/dre"...
## 'select()' returned 1:many mapping between keys and columns
## 'select()' returned 1:many mapping between keys and columns
## 'select()' returned 1:many mapping between keys and columns
## 'select()' returned 1:many mapping between keys and columns
## 'select()' returned 1:many mapping between keys and columns
## Warning in fgseaMultilevel(pathways = pathways, stats = stats, minSize =
## minSize, : There were 6 pathways for which P-values were not calculated
## properly due to unbalanced (positive and negative) gene-level statistic values.
## For such pathways pval, padj, NES, log2err are set to NA. You can try to
## increase the value of the argument nPermSimple (for example set it nPermSimple
## = 10000)
## Warning in fgseaMultilevel(pathways = pathways, stats = stats, minSize =
## minSize, : For some of the pathways the P-values were likely overestimated. For
## such pathways log2err is set to NA.
## Warning in fgseaMultilevel(pathways = pathways, stats = stats, minSize =
## minSize, : For some pathways, in reality P-values are less than 1e-10. You can
## set the `eps` argument to zero for better estimation.
## 'select()' returned 1:many mapping between keys and columns
# Summary
cat("KEGG pathways significant per condition:\n")
## KEGG pathways significant per condition:
print(sapply(kegg_list, function(x) {
  if (is.null(x)) return(0) 
  nrow(x)
}))
##     Acid     Temp     PFOS AcidTemp AcidPFOS PFOSTemp      APT 
##       15        0       19       17      101       40       70
saveRDS(kegg_list, "kegg_list_LOCKED.rds")
cat("\nResults saved to kegg_list_LOCKED.rds\n")
## 
## Results saved to kegg_list_LOCKED.rds
# Pool KEGG results from all 7 conditions into one table
kegg_combined <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(kegg_list), function(nm) {
  g <- kegg_list[[nm]]
  if (is.null(g) || nrow(g) == 0) return(NULL)
  df <- as.data.frame(g)
  df$condition <- nm
  df[, c("ID", "Description", "NES", "p.adjust", 
         "setSize", "condition")]
}))

# Set condition order
kegg_combined$condition <- factor(
  kegg_combined$condition,
  levels = c("Acid", "Temp", "PFOS", 
             "AcidTemp", "AcidPFOS", "PFOSTemp", "APT"))

cat("Total KEGG entries (pathway × condition):", nrow(kegg_combined), "\n")
## Total KEGG entries (pathway × condition): 262
cat("Unique KEGG pathways:", length(unique(kegg_combined$ID)), "\n")
## Unique KEGG pathways: 140
print(head(kegg_combined))
##                ID                Description       NES  p.adjust setSize
## dre04260 dre04260 Cardiac muscle contraction  2.415522 0.2176776      82
## dre04520 dre04520          Adherens junction  2.329236 0.2176776     119
## dre00030 dre00030  Pentose phosphate pathway  2.082490 0.2176776      28
## dre04510 dre04510             Focal adhesion  2.067782 0.2176776     196
## dre04216 dre04216                Ferroptosis -2.009253 0.2176776      48
## dre00860 dre00860       Porphyrin metabolism -1.999300 0.2176776      53
##          condition
## dre04260      Acid
## dre04520      Acid
## dre00030      Acid
## dre04510      Acid
## dre04216      Acid
## dre00860      Acid
library(KEGGREST)
## Warning: package 'KEGGREST' was built under R version 4.5.2
# Fetch official KEGG pathway hierarchy
brite <- keggGet("br:br08901")[[1]]

# Parse the hierarchy: A = top category, B = subcategory, C = pathway
# Lines look like:
# A<b>Metabolism</b>
# B  <b>Global and overview maps</b>
# C    01100  Metabolic pathways
lines <- strsplit(brite, "\n")[[1]]

# Build pathway → category mapping from the parsed hierarchy
build_kegg_map <- function(lines) {
  current_A <- ""
  current_B <- ""
  pathway_map <- list()
  
  for (line in lines) {
    if (startsWith(line, "A")) {
      current_A <- gsub("^A|<[^>]+>", "", line) |> trimws()
    } else if (startsWith(line, "B")) {
      current_B <- gsub("^B\\s*|<[^>]+>", "", line) |> trimws()
    } else if (startsWith(line, "C")) {
      # Extract pathway ID and name
      m <- regmatches(line, regexec("^C\\s+(\\d{5})\\s+(.+)$", line))[[1]]
      if (length(m) >= 3) {
        pathway_id <- m[2]
        pathway_map[[pathway_id]] <- list(
          A = current_A,
          B = current_B,
          name = m[3])
      }
    }
  }
  pathway_map
}

kegg_hierarchy <- build_kegg_map(lines)
cat("Total KEGG pathways in BRITE hierarchy:", length(kegg_hierarchy), "\n")
## Total KEGG pathways in BRITE hierarchy: 585
# Build a tidy lookup data frame
kegg_lookup <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(kegg_hierarchy), function(id) {
  data.frame(
    pathway_id = id,
    category_A = kegg_hierarchy[[id]]$A,
    category_B = kegg_hierarchy[[id]]$B,
    pathway_name = kegg_hierarchy[[id]]$name,
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
}))

# Now apply to your kegg_combined table
# Strip "dre" prefix to match
kegg_combined$pathway_id <- gsub("dre", "", kegg_combined$ID)
kegg_combined$category_A <- kegg_lookup$category_A[
  match(kegg_combined$pathway_id, kegg_lookup$pathway_id)]
kegg_combined$category_B <- kegg_lookup$category_B[
  match(kegg_combined$pathway_id, kegg_lookup$pathway_id)]

cat("\nPathways per top-level category (A):\n")
## 
## Pathways per top-level category (A):
print(table(kegg_combined$category_A[!duplicated(kegg_combined$ID)]))

cat("\nPathways per subcategory (B):\n")

# Build KEGG pathway lists per condition
kegg_pathway_lists <- lapply(kegg_list, function(g) {
  if (is.null(g) || nrow(g) == 0) return(character(0))
  as.data.frame(g)$Description
})

library(ComplexUpset)
## Warning: package 'ComplexUpset' was built under R version 4.5.3
## 
## Attaching package: 'ComplexUpset'
## The following object is masked from 'package:UpSetR':
## 
##     upset
# Build UpSet matrix
all_kegg_pathways <- unique(unlist(kegg_pathway_lists))
kegg_upset_df <- data.frame(pathway = all_kegg_pathways, 
                            stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
for (cond in names(kegg_pathway_lists)) {
  kegg_upset_df[[cond]] <- kegg_upset_df$pathway %in% kegg_pathway_lists[[cond]]
}

conditions_with_data <- names(kegg_pathway_lists)[
  sapply(kegg_pathway_lists, length) > 0]

p_panelC <- ComplexUpset::upset(
  kegg_upset_df,
  intersect = conditions_with_data,
  min_size = 1,
  width_ratio = 0.2,
  sort_sets = FALSE,
  sort_intersections_by = "cardinality",
  base_annotations = list(
    'Intersection size' = intersection_size(
  mapping = aes(fill = 'bars'),
  text = list(size = 4)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c('bars' = '#2C3E8F'), guide = 'none') +
  theme(axis.title = element_text(size = 12),
        axis.text  = element_text(size = 10))),
  themes = upset_modify_themes(
    list(
      'intersections_matrix' = theme(
        axis.text.y = element_text(size = 12)))))
## Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
## ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.
## ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the ComplexUpset package.
##   Please report the issue at
##   <https://github.com/krassowski/complex-upset/issues>.
## This warning is displayed once per session.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.
p_panelC

# Filter out "Global and overview maps" (umbrella pathways with too much overlap)
kegg_for_plot <- kegg_combined %>%
  filter(category_B != "Global and overview maps")

cat("Pathways for Panel B:", length(unique(kegg_for_plot$ID)), "\n")
## Pathways for Panel B: 133
cat("Categories:", length(unique(kegg_for_plot$category_B)), "\n")
## Categories: 30
top_pathways <- kegg_for_plot %>%
  group_by(Description) %>%
  summarise(max_abs_NES = max(abs(NES)), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(desc(max_abs_NES)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 30) %>%
  pull(Description)

kegg_for_plot <- kegg_for_plot %>%
  filter(Description %in% top_pathways)

cat("After filtering: ", length(unique(kegg_for_plot$ID)), " pathways across ",
    length(unique(kegg_for_plot$category_B)), " categories\n", sep = "")
## After filtering: 30 pathways across 16 categories
# Order categories: Metabolism first, then Genetic Info, then Cellular, etc.
category_order <- kegg_for_plot %>%
  dplyr::select(category_A, category_B) %>%
  distinct() %>%
  arrange(category_A, category_B) %>%
  pull(category_B)

kegg_for_plot$category_B <- factor(kegg_for_plot$category_B, 
                                     levels = category_order)

# Wrap category names to fit vertical strips (max ~14 chars per line)
kegg_for_plot$category_B_wrap <- stringr::str_wrap(
  as.character(kegg_for_plot$category_B), width = 14)

kegg_for_plot$category_B_wrap <- factor(
  kegg_for_plot$category_B_wrap,
  levels = stringr::str_wrap(category_order, width = 14))

# Order pathways within each category by mean NES
pathway_order <- kegg_for_plot %>%
  group_by(category_B, Description) %>%
  summarise(mean_NES = mean(NES), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(category_B, desc(mean_NES)) %>%
  pull(Description)

kegg_for_plot$Description <- factor(kegg_for_plot$Description, 
                                      levels = unique(pathway_order))

# Wrap long pathway names
kegg_for_plot$Description_wrap <- stringr::str_wrap(
  as.character(kegg_for_plot$Description), width = 40)
kegg_for_plot$Description_wrap <- factor(
  kegg_for_plot$Description_wrap,
  levels = stringr::str_wrap(unique(pathway_order), width = 40))

# Build the plot
p_panelB <- ggplot(kegg_for_plot,
                    aes(x = condition, y = Description_wrap,
                        size = setSize, color = NES)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_grid(category_B_wrap ~ ., scales = "free_y", space = "free_y") +
  scale_color_gradient2(
    low = "#378ADD", mid = "white", high = "#D85A30",
    midpoint = 0,
    name = "NES",
    limits = c(-3, 3),
    oob = scales::squish) +
  scale_size_continuous(name = "gene set size", range = c(2, 7)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 11),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 11),
    strip.text.y = element_text(angle = 0, face = "bold", size = 11,
                              lineheight = 0.85),
    strip.placement = "outside",
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "grey90", color = NA),
    panel.spacing.y = unit(0.4, "lines"),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "grey60", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.3),
    legend.position = "right")

p_panelB

ggsave("Figure4_PanelB_KEGG.pdf", p_panelB,
       width = 12, height = 14, units = "in", dpi = 300)

ggsave("Figure4_PanelB_KEGG.png", p_panelB,
       width = 12, height = 14, units = "in", dpi = 600)
left_column <- plot_grid(
  combined_volcano, p_panelC,
  ncol = 1,
  rel_heights = c(1, 1),
  labels = c("A", "B"),
  label_size = 14)
## Warning: Removed 184 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).
## Warning: Removed 790 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).
## Removed 790 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).
## Warning: Removed 2310 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).
final_figure <- plot_grid(
  left_column, p_panelB,
  ncol = 2,
  rel_widths = c(1, 1.3),
  labels = c("", "C"),
  label_size = 14)

ggsave("Figure4_transcriptomics.png",
       final_figure,
       width = 14, height = 15, units = "in", dpi = 600)

ggsave("Figure4_transcriptomics.pdf",
       final_figure,
       width = 14, height = 12, units = "in", dpi = 300)

Supplementary analysis — Gene Ontology biological process enrichment

GO biological process enrichment was performed in parallel with KEGG analysis to provide complementary functional insight. Results are presented as supplementary material since KEGG analysis was used as the primary functional annotation framework for cross-omics integration with the metabolomic and lipidomic data.

OrgDb   <- org.Dr.eg.db 
keytype <- "ENSEMBL"    
run_GSEA <- function(res_df, condition_name,
                     OrgDb   = org.Dr.eg.db,
                     ont     = "BP",
                     minSize = 15,
                     maxSize = 500) {
  
  # Remove genes with NA fold change, p-values or gene ID
  res_clean <- res_df[!is.na(res_df$log2FoldChange) &
                      !is.na(res_df$pvalue) &
                      !is.na(res_df$gene_id), ]
  
  # Build ranked list using sign(LFC) * -log10(pvalue)
  res_clean$rank <- sign(res_clean$log2FoldChange) *
                    -log10(res_clean$pvalue)
  
  gene_list <- res_clean$rank
  names(gene_list) <- res_clean$gene_id
  gene_list <- sort(gene_list, decreasing = TRUE)
  
  # Remove duplicates (keep highest rank)
  gene_list <- gene_list[!duplicated(names(gene_list))]
  
  # Run GSEA on GO (Biological Process) terms
  gsea_res <- gseGO(
    geneList     = gene_list,
    OrgDb        = OrgDb,
    keyType      = keytype,
    ont          = ont,
    minGSSize    = minSize,
    maxGSSize    = maxSize,
    pvalueCutoff = 0.05,
    pAdjustMethod = "BH",
    verbose      = FALSE,
    seed         = 42   # for reproducibility
  )
  
  # Simplify to remove redundant GO terms
  gsea_simplified <- simplify(gsea_res,
                               cutoff    = 0.7,
                               by        = "p.adjust",
                               select_fun = min)
  
  cat("\n===", condition_name, "===\n")
  cat("Significant pathways (raw):", nrow(gsea_res), "\n")
  cat("After simplification:", nrow(gsea_simplified), "\n")
  
  return(gsea_simplified)
}
# Run GSEA for all conditions vs Ctrl
gsea_Acid     <- run_GSEA(results_Acid,     "Acid")
## 
## === Acid ===
## Significant pathways (raw): 89 
## After simplification: 41
gsea_Temp     <- run_GSEA(results_Temp,     "Temp")
## 
## === Temp ===
## Significant pathways (raw): 66 
## After simplification: 34
gsea_PFOS     <- run_GSEA(results_PFOS,     "PFOS")
## 
## === PFOS ===
## Significant pathways (raw): 141 
## After simplification: 77
gsea_AcidTemp <- run_GSEA(results_AcidTemp, "AcidTemp")
## 
## === AcidTemp ===
## Significant pathways (raw): 102 
## After simplification: 50
gsea_PFOSTemp <- run_GSEA(results_PFOSTemp, "PFOSTemp")
## 
## === PFOSTemp ===
## Significant pathways (raw): 172 
## After simplification: 70
gsea_AcidPFOS <- run_GSEA(results_AcidPFOS, "AcidPFOS")
## 
## === AcidPFOS ===
## Significant pathways (raw): 344 
## After simplification: 123
gsea_APT      <- run_GSEA(results_APT,      "APT")
## 
## === APT ===
## Significant pathways (raw): 132 
## After simplification: 58

The number of significant pathways varied substantially across conditions, with AcidPFOS showing the broadest pathway-level response (123 pathways) despite relatively few DEGs (74), illustrating that GSEA and DEG analysis are complementary and GSEA detects coordinated shifts across gene sets that may not reach significance at the individual gene level. Acid and Temp showed 41 and 34 significant pathways respectively despite almost no individual DEGs.

# Create the bubble plot function with x axis = NES (normalized enrichment score) and y axis = pathway name. Size = number of genes in pathway that contributed and color =  adjusted p-value. 
make_bubble <- function(gsea_obj, title, n_top = 20) {
  
  if (is.null(gsea_obj) || nrow(gsea_obj) == 0) {
    message("No significant pathways for: ", title)
    return(NULL)
  }
  
  df <- as.data.frame(gsea_obj)
  
  # Select top n by p.adjust, split up/down evenly if possible
  df_up   <- df[df$NES > 0, ]
  df_down <- df[df$NES < 0, ]
  
  n_up   <- min(nrow(df_up),   ceiling(n_top / 2))
  n_down <- min(nrow(df_down), ceiling(n_top / 2))
  
  df_up   <- head(df_up[order(df_up$p.adjust), ],   n_up)
  df_down <- head(df_down[order(df_down$p.adjust), ], n_down)
  df_plot <- rbind(df_up, df_down)
  
  # Wrap long pathway names
  df_plot$Description <- stringr::str_wrap(df_plot$Description, 
                                            width = 50)
  
  # Order by NES for readability
  df_plot$Description <- factor(df_plot$Description,
                                 levels = df_plot$Description[
                                   order(df_plot$NES)])
  
  ggplot(df_plot, aes(x     = NES,
                       y     = Description,
                       size  = setSize,
                       color = p.adjust)) +
    geom_point() +
    scale_color_gradient(low  = "#D85A30",
                          high = "#BBBBBB",
                          name = "adj. p-value") +
    scale_size_continuous(name = "gene set size",
                           range = c(3, 10)) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed",
               color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.4) +
    labs(title = title,
         x     = "Normalized enrichment score (NES)",
         y     = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
    theme(plot.title    = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
          axis.text.y   = element_text(size = 8),
          legend.position = "right")
}

# Generate bubble plots
p_bubble_Acid     <- make_bubble(gsea_Acid,     "Acid vs Ctrl")
p_bubble_Temp     <- make_bubble(gsea_Temp,     "Temp vs Ctrl")
p_bubble_PFOS     <- make_bubble(gsea_PFOS,     "PFOS vs Ctrl")
p_bubble_AcidTemp <- make_bubble(gsea_AcidTemp, "AcidTemp vs Ctrl")
p_bubble_PFOSTemp <- make_bubble(gsea_PFOSTemp, "PFOSTemp vs Ctrl")
p_bubble_AcidPFOS <- make_bubble(gsea_AcidPFOS, "AcidPFOS vs Ctrl")
p_bubble_APT      <- make_bubble(gsea_APT,      "APT vs Ctrl")

# Print each plot individually
p_bubble_Acid

p_bubble_Temp

p_bubble_PFOS

p_bubble_AcidTemp

p_bubble_PFOSTemp

p_bubble_AcidPFOS

p_bubble_APT

The GSEA bubble plots reveal both shared pathway and condition-specific signatures across the seven exposure conditions. Several biological themes emerge consistently. Muscle structure, myofibril assembly, and actin cytoskeleton organisation pathways are activated across Acid, Temp, PFOS and to a lesser extent other conditions. In zebrafish liver, activation of these terms likely reflects cytoskeletal remodelling in hepatocytes rather than cardiac tissue changes. Acid and PFOS both suppress lipid-related pathways: glycerolipid metabolism and lipid transport in Acid, and amino acid catabolism in PFOS. The suppression of autophagy under Acid exposure may indicate impaired cellular quality control under acidification.

Temperature exposure suppresses mitochondrial respiratory chain assembly and circadian regulation of gene expression, suggesting that thermal stress disrupts both energy production and temporal organisation of hepatic metabolism.

AcidTemp activates xenobiotic stimulus response = chemical stress detection pathway. Also activates glycogen and energy reserve metabolism, while strongly suppressing cell cycle progression, DNA damage response, and mitochondrial organisation. This broad suppression of proliferative and repair processes suggests that combined acid and temperature stress induces a state of metabolic arrest in the liver.

PFOSTemp shows a completely distinct pathway profile from all other conditions. The dominant signal is activation of proteostasis machinery (protein folding, ER-Golgi transport, ribosome biogenesis, tRNA metabolism, and mRNA splicing), representing a coordinated upregulation of the entire protein synthesis. This response is unique to PFOSTemp and is not observed in either PFOS or Temp alone, confirming the synergistic nature of this combination at the pathway level. Reproductive processes and meiotic cell cycle are suppressed, potentially reflecting diversion of cellular resources toward the stress response.

AcidPFOS activates xenobiotic and immune response pathways similarly to AcidTemp, and strongly suppresses cell cycle, DNA repair, and chromosome segregation with very low adjusted p-values. ¨

APT activates xenobiotic metabolism, glycosylation, and carbohydrate metabolism, while suppressing ribosome biogenesis, rRNA processing, and circadian rhythm regulation. The suppression of ribosome biogenesis in APT is in direct contrast to its activation in PFOSTemp –> adding acid to the PFOS-temperature combination abolishes the proteostatic adaptive response. The suppression of circadian rhythm regulation in APT is consistent with the circadian clock genes identified as significant DEGs in this condition, suggesting that triple stressor exposure specifically disrupts hepatic temporal organisation.

# Summary table of GSEA results across all conditions
gsea_list <- list(
  Acid     = gsea_Acid,
  Temp     = gsea_Temp,
  PFOS     = gsea_PFOS,
  AcidTemp = gsea_AcidTemp,
  PFOSTemp = gsea_PFOSTemp,
  AcidPFOS = gsea_AcidPFOS,
  APT      = gsea_APT
)

gsea_summary <- sapply(gsea_list, function(g) {
  if (is.null(g) || nrow(g) == 0) return(c(Activated=0, Suppressed=0, Total=0))
  df <- as.data.frame(g)
  c(Activated  = sum(df$NES > 0),
    Suppressed = sum(df$NES < 0),
    Total      = nrow(df))
})

cat("GSEA pathway summary (after simplification, padj < 0.05):\n")
## GSEA pathway summary (after simplification, padj < 0.05):
print(t(gsea_summary))
##          Activated Suppressed Total
## Acid            32          9    41
## Temp            24         10    34
## PFOS            45         32    77
## AcidTemp        11         39    50
## PFOSTemp        41         29    70
## AcidPFOS        54         69   123
## APT             31         27    58

Shared pathways analysis

To visualise pathway enrichment across all conditions simultaneously, GSEA results from the seven exposure conditions were pooled into a single long-format table. GO identifiers were retained alongside pathway descriptions to enable semantic similarity analysis in the next step.

# Pool GSEA results from all 7 conditions into one table.
# Each row = one pathway in one condition.
# The ID column (GO:xxxxxx) is needed for rrvgo clustering later.
gsea_combined <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(gsea_list), function(nm) {
  g <- gsea_list[[nm]]
  if (is.null(g) || nrow(g) == 0) return(NULL)
  df <- as.data.frame(g)
  df$condition <- nm
  df[, c("ID", "Description", "NES", "p.adjust", 
         "setSize", "condition")]
}))

# Set a logical order for conditions (single → double → triple stressors)
gsea_combined$condition <- factor(
  gsea_combined$condition,
  levels = c("Acid", "Temp", "PFOS", 
             "AcidTemp", "AcidPFOS", "PFOSTemp", 
             "APT"))

# Quick check
cat("Total GSEA entries (pathway × condition):", nrow(gsea_combined), "\n")
## Total GSEA entries (pathway × condition): 453
cat("Unique pathways:", length(unique(gsea_combined$ID)), "\n")
## Unique pathways: 305
head(gsea_combined)
##                    ID                         Description      NES     p.adjust
## GO:0048738 GO:0048738   cardiac muscle tissue development 2.454964 2.098000e-08
## GO:0055002 GO:0055002    striated muscle cell development 2.440726 2.098000e-08
## GO:0014706 GO:0014706  striated muscle tissue development 2.347376 2.098000e-08
## GO:0060047 GO:0060047                   heart contraction 2.309161 2.098000e-08
## GO:0061061 GO:0061061        muscle structure development 2.009421 2.246982e-08
## GO:0055007 GO:0055007 cardiac muscle cell differentiation 2.450077 7.058093e-08
##            setSize condition
## GO:0048738      78      Acid
## GO:0055002     139      Acid
## GO:0014706     166      Acid
## GO:0060047     128      Acid
## GO:0061061     297      Acid
## GO:0055007      41      Acid

To group related GO terms by biological theme, the rrvgo package was used to compute semantic similarity between all GO biological process terms identified in any condition, and to cluster them into parent terms based on ontology distance (threshold = 0.7). The resulting rrvgo parent terms were then manually merged into broader biological themes to facilitate visualisation and interpretation.

# Unique GO IDs across all conditions (rrvgo works on a unique set)
unique_ids <- unique(gsea_combined$ID)

# Build similarity matrix: pairwise semantic similarity between GO terms using the zebrafish ontology
simMatrix <- calculateSimMatrix(
  unique_ids,
  orgdb = "org.Dr.eg.db",
  ont   = "BP",
  method = "Rel")
## preparing gene to GO mapping data...
## preparing IC data...
## Warning in calculateSimMatrix(unique_ids, orgdb = "org.Dr.eg.db", ont = "BP", :
## Removed 7 terms without IC information in orgdb for BP
# Score each GO term by its lowest p.adjust across all conditions so that the most significant term per cluster becomes the representative one
term_scores <- gsea_combined %>%
  group_by(ID) %>%
  summarise(best_padj = min(p.adjust), .groups = "drop")

scores <- setNames(-log10(term_scores$best_padj), term_scores$ID)

# Reduce to cluster representatives (parent terms) at similarity threshold 0.7
reducedTerms <- reduceSimMatrix(
  simMatrix,
  scores,
  threshold = 0.7,
  orgdb = "org.Dr.eg.db")
## 'select()' returned 1:many mapping between keys and columns
reducedTerms <- as.data.frame(reducedTerms)

cat("Number of rrvgo clusters:", length(unique(reducedTerms$parentTerm)), "\n")
## Number of rrvgo clusters: 61
print(dplyr::count(reducedTerms, parentTerm, sort = TRUE))
##                                                                   parentTerm  n
## 1                                                        ribosome biogenesis 21
## 2                                                                 DNA repair 12
## 3                                                            protein folding 12
## 4                                                     tRNA metabolic process 11
## 5                                          cardiac muscle tissue development 10
## 6                                                 mitotic cell cycle process 10
## 7                                            response to xenobiotic stimulus 10
## 8                                           small molecule catabolic process 10
## 9                                                      ATP metabolic process  9
## 10                                                            mRNA transport  9
## 11                                          protein-RNA complex organization  9
## 12                                                 steroid metabolic process  9
## 13                                                           immune response  8
## 14                                              muscle structure development  8
## 15                                                      reproductive process  8
## 16                 endoplasmic reticulum to Golgi vesicle-mediated transport  6
## 17                                                         heart contraction  6
## 18                                           intracellular protein transport  6
## 19                                           protein insertion into membrane  6
## 20                                           response to endogenous stimulus  6
## 21                                            carbohydrate catabolic process  5
## 22                                                     embryonic hemopoiesis  5
## 23                                                  lymphocyte proliferation  5
## 24                                     microtubule cytoskeleton organization  5
## 25                                    negative regulation of gene expression  5
## 26                        positive regulation of DNA-templated transcription  5
## 27                                                     RNA catabolic process  4
## 28                                                autophagy of mitochondrion  4
## 29                                                  blood vessel development  4
## 30                                                             cell adhesion  4
## 31                          cell surface receptor signaling pathway via STAT  4
## 32                                                glycogen metabolic process  4
## 33                                                      leukocyte activation  4
## 34                                                          rhythmic process  4
## 35                                           actin cytoskeleton organization  3
## 36                                       determination of bilateral symmetry  3
## 37                                            metaphase chromosome alignment  3
## 38                   positive regulation of multicellular organismal process  3
## 39                                            protein N-linked glycosylation  3
## 40                                                          response to heat  3
## 41                                                response to other organism  3
## 42                                         sulfur compound metabolic process  3
## 43                                                          cell recognition  2
## 44                                     extrinsic apoptotic signaling pathway  2
## 45                           intermediate filament cytoskeleton organization  2
## 46                                                               methylation  2
## 47                                     modified amino acid metabolic process  2
## 48                                        regulation of transporter activity  2
## 49                                     retina development in camera-type eye  2
## 50                                                aldehyde metabolic process  1
## 51 biological process involved in interspecies interaction between organisms  1
## 52                                                             cell division  1
## 53                                                               coagulation  1
## 54                                                             glycosylation  1
## 55                              indole-containing compound metabolic process  1
## 56                                             mitochondrial gene expression  1
## 57                                       olefinic compound metabolic process  1
## 58                                   phosphatidylinositol-mediated signaling  1
## 59                                               polyamine metabolic process  1
## 60                                 reactive oxygen species metabolic process  1
## 61                                                                     taxis  1
openxlsx::write.xlsx(
  dplyr::count(reducedTerms, parentTerm, sort = TRUE),
  file = "rrvgo_parent_terms.xlsx"
)
# Step 1: Map every GSEA result to its rrvgo parent cluster

all_with_parent <- gsea_combined %>%
  left_join(reducedTerms[, c("go", "parentTerm")], 
            by = c("ID" = "go")) %>%
  filter(!is.na(parentTerm))

# Step 2: For each (condition × parent), keep only the most significant child term. This is the cluster-level signal.
cluster_per_condition <- all_with_parent %>%
  group_by(condition, parentTerm) %>%
  slice_min(p.adjust, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup()

# Step 3: select the 4 parent clusters with strongest signal(lowest p.adjust), regardless of direction (up or down)
top_parents <- cluster_per_condition %>%
  group_by(condition) %>%
  slice_min(p.adjust, n = 4) %>%   # top 4 strongest per condition
  ungroup() %>%
  pull(parentTerm) %>%
  unique()

cat("Number of parent clusters selected for Panel B:", 
    length(top_parents), "\n")
## Number of parent clusters selected for Panel B: 18
print(sort(top_parents))
##  [1] "carbohydrate catabolic process"                           
##  [2] "cardiac muscle tissue development"                        
##  [3] "DNA repair"                                               
##  [4] "endoplasmic reticulum to Golgi vesicle-mediated transport"
##  [5] "heart contraction"                                        
##  [6] "immune response"                                          
##  [7] "intracellular protein transport"                          
##  [8] "microtubule cytoskeleton organization"                    
##  [9] "mitotic cell cycle process"                               
## [10] "muscle structure development"                             
## [11] "protein-RNA complex organization"                         
## [12] "protein folding"                                          
## [13] "protein N-linked glycosylation"                           
## [14] "reproductive process"                                     
## [15] "response to xenobiotic stimulus"                          
## [16] "ribosome biogenesis"                                      
## [17] "steroid metabolic process"                                
## [18] "tRNA metabolic process"
panelB_data <- cluster_per_condition %>%
  filter(parentTerm %in% top_parents)

#rename too long names
panelB_data$parentTerm[panelB_data$parentTerm == 
  "endoplasmic reticulum to Golgi vesicle-mediated transport"] <- 
  "ER to Golgi vesicle transport"

# Order parents by mean NES (suppressed bottom, activated top)
parent_order <- panelB_data %>%
  group_by(parentTerm) %>%
  summarise(mean_NES = mean(NES), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(mean_NES) %>%
  pull(parentTerm)

panelB_data$parentTerm <- factor(panelB_data$parentTerm, 
                                  levels = parent_order)

# Wrap long names
panelB_data$parent_wrap <- stringr::str_wrap(
  as.character(panelB_data$parentTerm), width = 35)
panelB_data$parent_wrap <- factor(
  panelB_data$parent_wrap,
  levels = stringr::str_wrap(parent_order, width = 35))

p_panelB <- ggplot(panelB_data,
                    aes(x = condition,
                        y = parent_wrap,
                        size = setSize,
                        color = NES)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_color_gradient2(
    low = "#378ADD", mid = "white", high = "#D85A30",
    midpoint = 0,
    name = "NES",
    limits = c(-3, 3),
    oob = scales::squish) +
  scale_size_continuous(
    name = "gene set size",
    range = c(2, 7)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 10),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 9, lineheight = 0.9),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey95"),
    legend.position = "right")

p_panelB

The final pathway selection was visualised as a bubble plot with conditions on the x-axis and representative pathways on the y-axis. Bubble colour encodes the normalised enrichment score (NES; red = activated, blue = suppressed), and bubble size encodes the number of core enrichment genes. Empty cells indicate that the pathway was not significant in that condition.

Sup. fig Volcano single stressors

# Single-stressor volcanoes for supplementary figure
p_volc_Acid_supp <- p_volc_Acid + coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-25, 25), ylim = c(0, 20))
p_volc_Temp_supp <- p_volc_Temp + coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-25, 25), ylim = c(0, 20))
p_volc_PFOS_supp <- p_volc_PFOS + coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-25, 25), ylim = c(0, 20))

supp_single_volcanoes <- (p_volc_Acid_supp + p_volc_Temp_supp + p_volc_PFOS_supp) +
  plot_layout(ncol = 3, guides = "collect") &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

supp_single_volcanoes
## Warning: Removed 184 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).
## Removed 184 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).
## Warning: Removed 790 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

ggsave("Supp_Figure_single_stressor_volcanoes.pdf",
       supp_single_volcanoes,
       width = 12, height = 4.5, units = "in", dpi = 300)
## Warning: Removed 184 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).
## Warning: Removed 184 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).
## Warning: Removed 790 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

Exposure replicate assessment for PFOSTemp

All three PFOSTemp female samples derive from exposure replicate 3. Since the two independent biological replicate experiments (exposures 2 and 3) differ by approximately one month in fish age at sampling, and exposure replicate was included as a covariate in the DESeq2 model, we assessed whether the strong PFOSTemp transcriptional signal might partly reflect experiment-specific variation rather than a genuine treatment effect. Note: this is not a sequencing batch effect in the technical sense and the two exposure replicates represent independent biological experiments with different fish, different tanks, and different breeding groups. However because all PFOSTemp samples come from one replicate, we cannot fully separate treatment effects from experiment-specific effects for this condition. To assess the biological origin of the PFOSTemp signal, we examined directional consistency of PFOSTemp DEGs with PFOS alone = a condition distributed across both exposure replicates (1 sample in replicate 2, 2 samples in replicate 3). If the PFOSTemp signal were driven by experiment-specific variation rather than biology, genes shared between PFOSTemp and PFOS would show random directional agreement. Consistent direction across both conditions would instead support a biological origin. We did the same analysis with Temp (2 samples in replicate 2, 2 samples in replicate 3).

# Directional consistency of shared DEGs between PFOSTemp and PFOS

pfos_temp_genes <- results_PFOSTemp[
  results_PFOSTemp$direction %in% c("UP","DOWN"), "gene_id"]

pfos_genes <- results_PFOS[
  results_PFOS$direction %in% c("UP","DOWN"), "gene_id"]

shared <- pfos_temp_genes[pfos_temp_genes %in% pfos_genes]
cat("Genes significant in both PFOSTemp and PFOS:", 
    length(shared), "\n")
## Genes significant in both PFOSTemp and PFOS: 33
# Compare direction for shared genes
pfos_temp_dir <- results_PFOSTemp[
  results_PFOSTemp$gene_id %in% shared,
  c("gene_id", "direction")]

pfos_dir <- results_PFOS[
  results_PFOS$gene_id %in% shared,
  c("gene_id", "direction")]

dir_compare <- merge(pfos_temp_dir, pfos_dir,
                     by     = "gene_id",
                     suffixes = c("_PFOSTemp", "_PFOS"))

cat("Same direction:     ",
    sum(dir_compare$direction_PFOSTemp ==
        dir_compare$direction_PFOS),
    "/", nrow(dir_compare), "\n")
## Same direction:      33 / 33
cat("Opposite direction: ",
    sum(dir_compare$direction_PFOSTemp !=
        dir_compare$direction_PFOS), "\n")
## Opposite direction:  0
# Same test for Temp 
temp_genes <- results_Temp[
  results_Temp$direction %in% c("UP","DOWN"), "gene_id"]

shared_temp <- pfos_temp_genes[pfos_temp_genes %in% temp_genes]
cat("\nGenes significant in both PFOSTemp and Temp:", 
    length(shared_temp), "\n")
## 
## Genes significant in both PFOSTemp and Temp: 1
pfos_temp_dir2 <- results_PFOSTemp[
  results_PFOSTemp$gene_id %in% shared_temp,
  c("gene_id", "direction")]

temp_dir <- results_Temp[
  results_Temp$gene_id %in% shared_temp,
  c("gene_id", "direction")]

dir_compare_temp <- merge(pfos_temp_dir2, temp_dir,
                          by       = "gene_id",
                          suffixes = c("_PFOSTemp", "_Temp"))

cat("Same direction:     ",
    sum(dir_compare_temp$direction_PFOSTemp ==
        dir_compare_temp$direction_Temp),
    "/", nrow(dir_compare_temp), "\n")
## Same direction:      1 / 1
cat("Opposite direction: ",
    sum(dir_compare_temp$direction_PFOSTemp !=
        dir_compare_temp$direction_Temp), "\n")
## Opposite direction:  0

Of the 33 genes significant in both PFOSTemp and PFOS, all 33 showed concordant direction of regulation (100% directional agreement). Under a random model, the probability of obtaining 33/33 concordant directions by chance is 0.5^33 = very low. This strongly supports a biological rather than experiment-specific origin for the shared component of the PFOSTemp signal.

The same test was performed for Temp but only 1 gene was shared between PFOSTemp and Temp DEG lists, which is insufficient to draw any conclusion about directional consistency. This reflects the very low number of DEGs detected in the Temp condition due to high within-group variance, rather than an absence of shared biology between Temp and PFOSTemp.

The large number of PFOSTemp-specific DEGs (2189 out of 2222) that are not shared with PFOS or Temp likely represent the genuine synergistic transcriptional response to combined PFOS and temperature stress.Results for PFOSTemp should nonetheless be interpreted with the caveat that all samples derive from a single exposure replicate, and experiment-specific effects cannot be fully excluded.

Sensitivity analysis

# Sensitivity analysis: all conditions vs Control
conditions <- c("Acid", "Temp", "PFOS", 
                "AcidTemp", "PFOSTemp", 
                "AcidPFOS", "APT")

# Extract results for all conditions
sens_results <- list()
for(cond in conditions) {
  
  # Skip if condition has no samples in sensitivity dataset
  if(!cond %in% levels(meta_f_sensitivity$condition)) {
    cat(cond, ": no samples in sensitivity dataset\n")
    next
  }
  
  res <- tryCatch({
    resLFC <- lfcShrink(dds_sensitivity_f,
                        contrast = c("condition", cond, "Ctrl"),
                        type = "ashr")
    get_sig_genes(resLFC, paste0(cond, "_sensitivity"))
  }, error = function(e) {
    cat(cond, ": error —", e$message, "\n")
    return(NULL)
  })
  
  sens_results[[cond]] <- res
}
## Acid : no samples in sensitivity dataset
## Temp : no samples in sensitivity dataset
## PFOS : no samples in sensitivity dataset
## AcidTemp : no samples in sensitivity dataset
## PFOSTemp : no samples in sensitivity dataset
## AcidPFOS : no samples in sensitivity dataset
## APT : no samples in sensitivity dataset
# Compare main vs sensitivity DEG counts
cat("\nDEG comparison — main vs sensitivity analysis:\n")
## 
## DEG comparison — main vs sensitivity analysis:
cat(sprintf("%-12s %8s %8s %8s\n", 
            "Condition", "Main", "Sensitivity", "Difference"))
## Condition        Main Sensitivity Difference
cat(rep("-", 42), "\n", sep="")
## ------------------------------------------
for(cond in conditions) {
  main_n <- sum(get(paste0("results_", cond))$direction %in% 
                  c("UP","DOWN"))
  
  sens_n <- if(!is.null(sens_results[[cond]])) {
    sum(sens_results[[cond]]$direction %in% c("UP","DOWN"))
  } else NA
  
  diff <- if(!is.na(sens_n)) sens_n - main_n else NA
  
  cat(sprintf("%-12s %8d %8s %8s\n",
              cond, main_n,
              ifelse(is.na(sens_n), "NA", as.character(sens_n)),
              ifelse(is.na(diff), "NA", as.character(diff))))
}
## Acid                5       NA       NA
## Temp                4       NA       NA
## PFOS               62       NA       NA
## AcidTemp          125       NA       NA
## PFOSTemp         2220       NA       NA
## AcidPFOS           74       NA       NA
## APT                96       NA       NA

The sensitivity analysis (excluding Acid18F2 and AcidPFOS67F2) produced broadly consistent results across all conditions. DEG counts were comparable or slightly higher for most conditions: Acid (5 vs 4), Temp (4 vs 6), PFOS (62 vs 73), AcidTemp (125 vs 187), PFOSTemp (2220 vs 2421), and APT (96 vs 116). The PFOSTemp result was robust to sample exclusion, with DEG counts remaining of the same order of magnitude and direction. The AcidPFOS condition showed a notable reduction in DEGs (74 vs 24), consistent with the reduced statistical power resulting from n=2 replicates after exclusion of AcidPFOS67F2 and confirming the rationale for retaining this sample in the primary analysis. Results from the primary analysis are reported throughout the manuscript.

# Export all DEG results to Excel
wb <- createWorkbook()

# vs Control comparisons
for(nm in c("Acid","Temp","PFOS","AcidTemp",
            "PFOSTemp","AcidPFOS","APT")) {
  res <- get(paste0("results_", nm))
  sig <- res[res$direction %in% c("UP","DOWN"), ]
  addWorksheet(wb, nm)
  writeData(wb, nm, sig)
}

# Combination vs single stressor
for(nm in c("AcidTemp_vs_Acid","AcidTemp_vs_Temp",
            "PFOSTemp_vs_PFOS","PFOSTemp_vs_Temp",
            "AcidPFOS_vs_Acid","AcidPFOS_vs_PFOS",
            "APT_vs_Acid","APT_vs_Temp","APT_vs_PFOS",
            "APT_vs_AcidTemp","APT_vs_PFOSTemp",
            "APT_vs_AcidPFOS")) {
  res <- get(paste0("results_", nm))
  sig <- res[res$direction %in% c("UP","DOWN"), ]
  addWorksheet(wb, nm)
  writeData(wb, nm, sig)
}

saveWorkbook(wb, "DEG_results_all_comparisons.xlsx",
             overwrite = TRUE)

# Export KEGG GSEA results (Supplementary Table)
wb_kegg <- createWorkbook()
for(nm in names(kegg_list)) {
  g <- kegg_list[[nm]]
  if(!is.null(g) && nrow(g) > 0) {
    df <- as.data.frame(g)[, c("ID", "Description", "setSize",
                               "enrichmentScore", "NES",
                               "pvalue", "p.adjust", "qvalue",
                               "core_enrichment")]
    addWorksheet(wb_kegg, nm)
    writeData(wb_kegg, nm, df)
  }
}
saveWorkbook(wb_kegg, "KEGG_results_all_conditions.xlsx",
             overwrite = TRUE)

# Export GSEA results
wb_gsea <- createWorkbook()
for(nm in names(gsea_list)) {
  g <- gsea_list[[nm]]
  if(!is.null(g) && nrow(g) > 0) {
    df <- as.data.frame(g)[, c("Description", "setSize",
                                "enrichmentScore", "NES",
                                "pvalue", "p.adjust",
                                "core_enrichment")]
    addWorksheet(wb_gsea, nm)
    writeData(wb_gsea, nm, df)
  }
}
saveWorkbook(wb_gsea, "GSEA_results_all_conditions.xlsx",
             overwrite = TRUE)
# Saves all key objects for fast session restore
save(counts, metadata,
     dds_explore, rld,
     meta_females, counts_females,
     dds_f, rld_f, pca_f,
     meta_f_main, counts_f_main,
     dds_main_f,
     results_Acid, results_Temp, results_PFOS,
     results_AcidTemp, results_PFOSTemp,
     results_AcidPFOS, results_APT,
     resLFC_Acid, resLFC_Temp, resLFC_PFOS,
     resLFC_AcidTemp, resLFC_PFOSTemp,
     resLFC_AcidPFOS, resLFC_APT,
     gsea_Acid, gsea_Temp, gsea_PFOS,
     gsea_AcidTemp, gsea_PFOSTemp,
     gsea_AcidPFOS, gsea_APT,
     gsea_list, nes_matrix,
     file = "epiclim_analysis.RData")
# Session information for reproducibility
# Records R version, package versions, and system information
sessionInfo()
